studyMetadata.description1: This first volume invites pastors’ wives to lay down the weight of performance and return to the bedrock of who they are in Christ. Beginning with the foundational question of identity — not what you do, but who you are — it moves through the intimacy of walking with Jesus, the beauty and complexity of marriage as partnership, and the hidden interior life that God sees and tends. It does not shy away from the hard terrain: the loneliness, the criticism, the moments of failure that ministry life so often brings. And it closes not with a call to do more, but with an invitation to rest — to discover that sustainability is not laziness but wisdom, and that a woman who knows her own name before God is far better equipped to serve, love, and lead than one who has lost herself in her role.
studyMetadata.description2: Wannan juzu’i na farko yana kiran matan fastoci da su ajiye nauyin nuna bajinta su koma ga asalin wacece su a cikin Almasihu. An fara ne da tambayar asali game da wayewar kai — ba abin da kuke yi ba, amma wacece ku — sannan ya tafi ga kusanci na tafiya tare da Yesu, kyawu da sarƙaƙiyar aure a matsayin abokan tarayya, da kuma rayuwar ciki ɓoyayye wacce Allah yake gani kuma yake kula da ita. Ba ya tsoron magana kan mawuyacin yanayi: kadaici, suka, da lokutan gazawa waɗanda rayuwar hidima take yawan kawowa. Kuma ba a rufe shi da kiran a ƙara yin aiki ba, amma da gayyata zuwa ga hutu — don gano cewa dawwama ba rago ba ne amma hikima ce, kuma macen da ta san sunanta na kansa a gaban Allah tana da cikakken shiri don yin hidima, ƙauna, da jagoranci fiye da wadda ta ɓata kanta a cikin matsayinta.
studyMetadata.description3: Ngal juzu’i arangal ɗon nodda rewɓe pastor’en ngam ɓe njippina teddeendi kuugal ngal ɓe ngadata, ɓe koota to asalu mo ɓe ngoni nder Almasiihu. Ɗum ɗon fuɗɗira bee ƴamol asalu dow annditugol hoore — naa ɗume a waɗata, amma moye a woni — nden ɗum yaha to ɓadtotooɗal yahdu bee Yeesu, booɗɗum e caɗeele teegal bana kuuɗe kawtal, e ngendam nder yeerteru ɗam Allah yi’ata e kakkilanta. Walaa hultungo wolwugo dow nokkuuje caɗtuɗe: kankoore, aybinande, e sa’aaji ronkere ɗi ngendam kuugal Allah ɓuri addugo. Ɗum hadataa bee noddaandu ngam waɗugo kuuɗe feere, amma bee noddaandu to siwtare — ngam anndugo wondugo dow laawol naa hulyere on, amma hikma on, nden debbo annduɗo innde mum e hoore mum yeeso Allah ɓuri heboyugo baawɗe ngam waɗugo kuugal, yiide, e ardaaki dow debbo majjuɗo hoore mum nder daraja mum.

theme.theme-annotation: Terracotta study theme — warm ochre, burnt sienna, and hillside green palette. Grounded and vibrant, evoking African earth tones, harvest, and communal labour. Suited to studies on identity, calling, womanhood, or vocation. Warm and energising rather than restful.

elements.question1: It’s worth having these passages open as you work through this session.

elements.text1:
Ngozi had been a pastor’s wife for eleven years. She could tell you the order of service by heart. She knew which women needed a visit, which families were struggling, which deacons needed managing. She ran the women’s fellowship, organised the children’s Christmas programme, led the choir practice when the choir director was absent, and made sure there was always food after the evening service.

She was, by every measure, excellent at the job.

But one Tuesday morning, sitting in the empty church after everyone had left, she realised something had gone quietly wrong. She could not remember the last time she had simply sat with Jesus — not as a pastor’s wife preparing for a meeting, not as a leader looking for a verse to share, but as herself. As Ngozi.

Who am I, she wondered, when I am not being useful?

That is the question this session begins with. Not “What does a pastor’s wife do?” but something more foundational: Who are you — to God?
Part 1 — The Danger of Performing

There is a particular pressure that comes with being married to a pastor. The congregation has expectations. The wider community has expectations. Sometimes the denomination has expectations. And often, underneath all of those, a woman has quietly built up a set of expectations for herself — a standard she feels she must meet in order to be worthy of the role.

This is not unique to Nigeria, but it takes on particular shape in traditional contexts. A pastor’s wife may feel she must dress a certain way, speak a certain way, raise perfect children, never show weakness, and always be available. If she falls short of any of these — even in small ways — she may feel not just inadequate, but unfaithful.

The Bible has a word for building your identity on your performance. Jesus used it of the Pharisees.

elements.passageText1: <sup>1</sup>Be careful not to display your righteousness merely to be seen by people. Otherwise you have no reward with your Father in heaven. <sup>2</sup>Thus whenever you do charitable giving, do not blow a trumpet before you, as the hypocrites do in synagogues and on streets so that people will praise them. I tell you the truth, they have their reward! ... <sup>5</sup>Whenever you pray, do not be like the hypocrites, because they love to pray while standing in synagogues and on street corners so that people can see them. Truly I say to you, they have their reward!

elements.question1: Jesus warns against doing good things in order to be seen doing them. In what ways can a pastor’s wife fall into this same trap — even with genuinely good intentions?
Jesus says those who perform for an audience “have received their reward in full.” What do you think he means? What is the reward — and what is lost?

deeper.question1: Can you think of a time when you did something good in ministry, but your motivation was more about how it looked than about love for God or love for people? What did that feel like?

elements.term1: “A good pastor’s wife must always be strong.”
elements.question1: Have you ever heard — or believed — that showing weakness as a pastor’s wife is a kind of failure? Where do you think that idea comes from?
elements.answer1:
The Bible never calls any believer to project strength they do not have. In fact, the apostle Paul says the opposite: “When I am weak, then I am strong” (2 Corinthians 12:10). The expectation of unbroken strength is not a biblical standard — it is often a cultural one, sometimes reinforced by church tradition, sometimes by what we see modelled around us.

A pastor’s wife who hides her struggles does not protect the congregation. She teaches them, by example, that the Christian life is about appearances. And she cuts herself off from the grace, prayer, and support she genuinely needs.

Strength that comes from Christ is a real and wonderful thing. But it is not the same as never admitting difficulty. Jesus wept (John 11:35). Paul listed his sufferings honestly (2 Corinthians 11:24-28). The Bible is consistently honest about human weakness — because the gospel is for weak people.

elements.text1: Part 2 — What Jesus Thought of Women

Before we can talk about who you are as a pastor’s wife, we need to talk about who you are to Jesus. And to understand that, it helps to notice how Jesus actually treated women — because it was not what his culture expected.

In first-century Jewish and Roman society, women had limited public standing. They could not testify in court. They were not formally educated in the Torah. Their place was in the household, not in public religious life.

Jesus stepped straight across all of that.

elements.passageText1: <sup>38</sup>Now as they went on their way, Jesus entered a certain village where a woman named Martha welcomed him as a guest. <sup>39</sup>She had a sister named Mary, who sat at the Lord’s feet and listened to what he said. <sup>40</sup>But Martha was distracted with all the preparations she had to make, so she came up to him and said, “Lord, don’t you care that my sister has left me to do all the work alone? Tell her to help me.” <sup>41</sup>But the Lord answered her, “Martha, Martha, you are worried and troubled about many things, <sup>42</sup>but one thing is needed. Mary has chosen the best part; it will not be taken away from her.”
elements.passageText2: <sup>38</sup>Suna tafiya, sai ya shiga wani ƙauye, wata mace kuma mai suna Marta ta sauke da shi a gidanta. <sup>39</sup>Tana kuwa da ’yar’uwa mai suna Maryamu, wadda ta zauna a gaban Ubangiji tana sauraron magana tasa. <sup>40</sup>Marta kuwa yawan hidimomi ya ɗauke mata hankali, sai ta je gunsa, ta ce, “Ya Ubangiji, ba ka kula ba, ‘yar’uwata ta bar ni ina hidima ni kaɗai? Gaya mata ta taimake ni mana.” <sup>41</sup>Amma Ubangiji ya amsa mata ya ce, “Marta, Marta! Hankalinki a tashe yake, kina kuma damuwa kan abu da yawa. <sup>42</sup>Bukatu kima ne, ta ainihi ɗaya ce. Maryamu ta zaɓi abu mai kyau, ba kuwa za a karɓe mata ba.”
elements.passageText3: <sup>38</sup>Yesu da almajiransa suna cikin tafiya ke nan, sai ya kai wani ƙauye inda wata mace mai suna Marta ta karɓe shi a gidanta. <sup>39</sup>Ita Marta tana da ˈyarˈuwa mai suna Maryamu, wadda ta zauna kurkusa da Ubangiji tana jin maganarsa. 10.38-39 Yoh 11.1 <sup>40</sup>Marta kuwa aikin abinci ya ɗauke mata hankali, sai ta je wurin Yesu, ta ce, “Ubangiji, ba ka damu da yadda ˈyarˈuwata ta bar mini yin abinci ni kaɗai ba? Faɗa mata ta zo ta taimake ni mana.” <sup>41</sup>Amma Ubangiji ya amsa mata ya ce, “Marta, Marta, hankalinki ya tashi, kin kuma damu a kan abubuwa da yawa. <sup>42</sup>Amma abu ɗaya tak ake bukata. 10.42 Abu ɗaya tak ake bukata: Waɗansu kofe na rubuce-rubucen hannu na dā suna da, “Abubuwa kaɗan ne na musamman” ne a nan. Maryamu ta riga ta zaɓi abu mai kyau, wanda ba za a iya karɓe mata ba.”

elements.question1: Martha is busy doing important and necessary things. Mary is sitting at Jesus’ feet — the posture of a student being taught by a rabbi. In Jesus’ world, that posture was for men, not women. What does Jesus say about Mary’s choice — and what does this tell you about how he sees women?
Martha feels that her busyness is not being appreciated — and she asks Jesus to take her side. His answer is gentle, but it is a redirection. What do you think he wants Martha to understand?

deeper.question1: Is there a “Martha” pattern in your own life — a tendency to let serving crowd out sitting with Jesus? What would it take to protect time for the “one thing that is needed”?

elements.text1: The same pattern appears at the resurrection. When Jesus rose from the dead, the first person he appeared to and the first person he sent with the news of the resurrection was not Peter, not John — it was a woman.

elements.passageText1:
1 Now very early on the first day of the week,[a] while it was still dark, Mary Magdalene[b] came to the tomb and saw that the stone had been moved away from the entrance.[c] 2 So she went running[d] to Simon Peter and the other disciple whom Jesus loved and told them, “They have taken the Lord from the tomb, and we don’t know where they have put him!” 3 Then Peter and the other disciple set out to go to the tomb.[e] 4 The two were running together, but the other disciple ran faster than Peter[f] and reached the tomb first.[g] 5 He bent down[h] and saw the strips of linen cloth lying there,[i] but he did not go in. 6 Then Simon Peter, who had been following him, arrived and went right into the tomb. He saw[j] the strips of linen cloth lying there, 7 and the face cloth,[k] which had been around Jesus’ head, not lying with the strips of linen cloth but rolled up in a place by itself.[l] 8 Then the other disciple, who had reached the tomb first, came in, and he saw and believed. 9 (For they did not yet understand[n] the scripture that Jesus[o] must rise from the dead.)

10 So the disciples went back to their homes. 11 But Mary stood outside the tomb weeping. As she wept, she bent down and looked into the tomb. 12 And she saw two angels in white sitting where Jesus’ body had been lying, one at the head and one at the feet. 13 They said[q] to her, “Woman,[r] why are you weeping?” Mary replied,[s] “They have taken my Lord away, and I do not know where they have put him!” 14 When she had said this, she turned around and saw Jesus standing there,[t] but she did not know that it was Jesus.

15 Jesus said to her, “Woman, why are you weeping? Who are you looking for?” Because she[u] thought he was the gardener, she said to him, “Sir, if you have carried him away, tell me where you have put him, and I will take him.” 16 Jesus said to her, “Mary.” She[v] turned and said to him in Aramaic,[w] “Rabboni”[x] (which means Teacher).[y] 17 Jesus replied,[z] “Do not touch me, for I have not yet ascended to my Father. Go to my brothers and tell them, ‘I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God.’” 18 Mary Magdalene came and informed the disciples, “I have seen the Lord!” And she told them what Jesus had said to her.
elements.passageText2: <sup>1</sup>To, a ranar farko ta mako, Maryamu Magadaliya ta je kabarin da asuba, tun da sauran duhu, ta ga an kawar da dutsen daga kabarin. <sup>2</sup>Sai ta yiwo gudu wurin Bitrus da kuma almajirin nan da Yesu yake ƙauna, ta ce musu, “An ɗauke Ubangiji daga kabarin, ba mu kuwa san inda aka sa shi ba.” <sup>3</sup>Sai Bitrus da almajirin nan suka fita suka doshi kabarin. <sup>4</sup>Duka biyu suka ruga a guje, amma almajirin nan ya tsere wa Bitrus, ya riga isa kabarin. <sup>5</sup>Yana duƙawa yana leƙa ciki, sai ya ga likkafanin lilin a ajiye, amma fa bai shiga ba. <sup>6</sup>Sa’an nan Bitrus ya zo a bayansa, ya kuwa shiga kabarin, ya ga likkafanin a ajiye, <sup>7</sup>mayanin kuma da yake kansa ba a ajiye tare da likkafanin yake ba, amma a naɗe shi kaɗai a waje ɗaya. <sup>8</sup>Sai almajirin nan da ya riga isa kabarin shi ma ya shiga, ya gani, ya kuma ba da gaskiya. <sup>9</sup>Don kafin lokacin nan ba su fahimci Nassin nan ba, cewa lalle ne yă tashi daga matattu. <sup>10</sup>Sai almajiran suka koma gida. <sup>11</sup>Maryamu kuwa tana tsaye a bakin kabarin daga waje, tana kuka. Tana cikin kuka sai ta duƙa, ta leƙa kabarin, <sup>12</sup>sai ta ga mala’iku biyu saye da fararen tufafi a zaune inda dā gawar Yesu take, ɗaga wajen kai, ɗaya kuwa wajen ƙafafu. <sup>13</sup>Suka ce mata, “Uwargida, don me kike kuka?” Ta ce musu, “Ai, an ɗauke Ubangijina, ban kuwa san inda aka sa shi ba.” <sup>14</sup>Da ta faɗi haka sai ta juya ta ga Yesu tsaye, amma ba ta gane Yesu ne ba. <sup>15</sup>Yesu ya ce mata, “Uwargida, don me kike kuka? Wa kuma kike nema?” Saboda kuma ta zaci shi ne mai aikin lambun, ta ce masa, “Maigida, in kai ne ka ɗauke shi, gaya mini inda ka sa shi mana, ni kuwa in ɗauke shi.” <sup>16</sup>Yesu ya ce mata, “Maryamu!” Sai ta juya ta ce masa da Yahudanci, “Rabboni!” wato Malam. <sup>17</sup>Yesu ya ce mata, “Kada ki riƙe ni, domin har yanzu ban hau wurin Uba ba tukuna. Sai dai ki tafi wurin ’yan’uwana ki ce musu ina hawa wurin Ubana kuma Ubanku, Allahna kuma Allahnku.” <sup>18</sup>Sai Maryamu Magadaliya ta je ta ce wa almajiran, “Na ga Ubangiji!” Ta kuma ce shi ya faɗa mata waɗannan abubuwa.
elements.passageText3: <sup>1</sup>Tun da sassafe a rana ta farko, a lokacin da gari bai gama wayewa ba, Maryamu ˈyar garin Magadala ta je kabarin Yesu, ta ga an ture dutsen da ya rufe bakin kabarin. <sup>2</sup>Sai ta ruga da gudu zuwa wurin Bitrus da kuma almajirin nan da Yesu yake ƙauna, ta ce musu, “Ai, an ɗauki Ubangiji daga kabarin, ba mu kuwa san inda aka sa shi ba.” <sup>3</sup>Sai Bitrus da almajirin nan suka fita suka kama hanya zuwa kabarin. <sup>4</sup>Duka biyun kuwa suka ruga da gudu, amma almajirin nan ya wuce Bitrus, ya riga Bitrus kai wurin kabarin. <sup>5</sup>Da ya sunkuya ya leƙa sai ya ga yadin lilin a ajiye, amma bai shiga cikin kabarin ba. <sup>6</sup>Saˈan nan Bitrus ya zo a bayansa, ya kuwa shiga cikin kabarin, ya ga yadin lilin a ajiye. <sup>7</sup>Ya kuma ga yadin da aka naɗe kewaye da kan Yesu ajiye a wani wuri dabam da yadin lilin. <sup>8</sup>Sai almajirin nan da ya riga Bitrus kai wurin kabarin shi ma ya shiga. Da ya gani sai ya ba da gaskiya. <sup>9</sup>Amma har yanzu dai ba su gane abin da Rubutacciyar Maganar Allah ta faɗa ba cewa dole ne Yesu yă sāke tashi daga matattu. <sup>10</sup>Sai almajiran suka koma gida. <sup>11</sup>Amma Maryamu ta tsaya a bakin kabarin daga waje, tana ta kuka. Tana cikin kuka ke nan, sai ta sunkuya ta leƙa cikin kabarin, <sup>12</sup>sai ta ga malaˈiku biyu da fararen tufafi suna zaune a inda gawar Yesu take a dā. Malaˈika ɗaya yana zaune ta wajen kai, ɗayan kuma ta wajen ƙafafu. <sup>13</sup>Sai malaˈikun suka tambaye ta suka ce, “Uwargida, me ya sa kike kuka?” <sup>14</sup>Da ta gama faɗin wannan magana sai ta juya ta ga Yesu a tsaye, amma ba ta gane cewa shi ne ba. <sup>15</sup>Sai Yesu ya tambaye ta ya ce, “Uwargida, me ya sa kike kuka? Wa kike nema?” <sup>16</sup>Yesu ya kira ta ya ce, “Maryamu!” <sup>17</sup>Yesu ya ce mata, “Kada ki riƙe ni, domin ban hau wurin Uba ba tukuna. Amma ki tafi wurin ˈyanˈuwana ki ce musu zan koma wurin Ubana kuma Ubanku, Allahna kuma Allahnku.” <sup>18</sup>Sai Maryamu ˈyar garin Magadala ta je ta ce wa almajiran, “Ai, na ga Ubangiji.” Ta kuma gaya musu abin da Yesu ya faɗa mata.

elements.question1: Mary Magdalene is the first witness and the first herald of the resurrection. In a culture where women’s testimony was doubted, Jesus deliberately chose her. What does this say about how God values women — and how he values you?
Mary recognises Jesus when he calls her by name (v16). She is known — personally, specifically, by name. How does it change the way you feel about your identity to know that Jesus knows you by name?

deeper.question1: The risen Jesus could have appeared to the disciples first, to the high priest, or to a crowd. He appeared first to a grieving woman, alone in a garden. What does that choice reveal about the kind of God he is?

elements.text1: Part 3 — Your Identity in Christ

The New Testament is bold about what happens to a person when they come to faith in Jesus. Your status before God is not based on your role, your family, your performance, or the expectations of your congregation. It is based entirely on what God has done for you in Christ.

This is not a minor theological point. It is the foundation on which everything else in your life — including your ministry — must be built. When that foundation is wrong, everything built on top of it becomes unstable.

elements.passageText1: 26 For in Christ Jesus you are all sons of God through faith.[a] 27 For all of you who[b] were baptized into Christ have clothed yourselves with Christ. 28 There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave[c] nor free, there is neither male nor female[d]—for all of you are one in Christ Jesus.
elements.passageText2: <sup>26</sup>Domin dukanku ’ya’yan Allah ne, ta wurin bangaskiya ga Almasihu Yesu. <sup>27</sup>Duk ɗaukacinku da aka yi wa baftisma ga bin Almasihu, kun ɗauki halin Almasihu ke nan. <sup>28</sup>Ba sauran cewa Bayahude ko Ba’al’umme, ko ɗa, ko bawa, ko namiji ko mace. Ai, dukkanku ɗaya kuke, na Almasihu Yesu.
elements.passageText3: <sup>26</sup>Gama ta wurin bangaskiya ga Almasihu Yesu, dukanku kun zama ˈyaˈyan Allah. <sup>27</sup>An kuma yi muku wankan baftisma wadda ta nuna cewa ku na Almasihu ne, saboda haka kun kuma ɗauki halin Almasihu, ya zama halinku. <sup>28</sup>Saboda haka, ba bambanci tsakanin Yahudawa da waɗanda ba Yahudawa ba. Ba kuma bambanci tsakanin ɗa da bawa, ko tsakanin mace da namiji. Ai, dukanku ɗaya ne cikin Almasihu Yesu.

elements.question1: Paul says that in Christ there is “neither male nor female.” He is not saying that differences disappear — he is saying that those differences no longer determine your standing before God. What does it mean for your sense of worth that your identity in Christ does not depend on your gender, your ethnicity, or your social position?

elements.passageText1: 3 Blessed[a] is[b] the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, who has blessed[c] us with every spiritual blessing in the heavenly realms in Christ. 4 For[d] he chose us in Christ[e] before the foundation of the world that we should be holy and blameless[f] before him[g] in love.[h] 5 He did this by predestining[i] us to adoption as his[j] legal heirs[k] through Jesus Christ, according to the pleasure[l] of his will— 6 to the praise of the glory of his grace[m] that he has freely bestowed on us in his dearly loved Son.
elements.passageText2: <sup>3</sup>Yabo yā tabbata ga Allahn Ubangijinmu Yesu Almasihu, da Ubansa kuma, wanda ya yi mana albarka da kowace albarka ta Ruhu, basamaniya, a cikin Almasihu, <sup>4</sup>domin kuwa ya zaɓe mu a cikin Almasihu tun ba a halicci duniya ba, mu zama tsarkaka, marasa aibu a gabansa. <sup>5</sup>Ya ƙaddara mu mu zama ’ya’yansa ta wurin Yesu Almasihu, bisa ga nufinsa na alheri, <sup>6</sup>domin mu yabi ɗaukakar alherinsa wanda ya ba mu kyauta hannu sake saboda Ƙaunataccensa.
elements.passageText3: <sup>3</sup>Albarka ta tabbata ga Allah, Uban Ubangijinmu Yesu Almasihu! Shi ne Allah wanda ta wurin Almasihu ya albarkace mu da kowace albarka ta ruhaniya cikin mulkinsa a sammai. <sup>4</sup>Ya zaɓe mu ta wurin zamanmu ɗaya da Almasihu tun ba a halicci duniya ba, domin mu zama masu tsarki, marasa laifi a gabansa. <sup>5</sup>Allah ya riga ya ƙaddara mu mu zama ˈyaˈyansa ta wurin Yesu Almasihu, bisa ga nufinsa na alheri. <sup>6</sup>Ya yi haka domin mu yabi darajar alherinsa da ya ba mu kyauta a yalwace ta wurin Ɗansa da yake ƙauna.

elements.question1: Paul heaps up three rich words: chosen, adopted, and accepted (“to the praise of his glorious grace”). These are not things you achieved — they are things done to you, for you, before you could do anything to deserve them. How does this shape the way you think about your value to God?

If your identity is already secure in Christ — chosen, adopted, fully accepted — what difference should that make to the way you handle criticism from the congregation, or the feeling that you are never doing enough?

deeper.question1: Ephesians 1:4 says God chose you “before the foundation of the world.” Your calling as a daughter of God is older than your husband’s calling to ministry. How does that change the way you think about your relationship to his role?

elements.term1: “My identity is tied to my husband’s ministry.”
elements.question1: Is it wrong for a pastor’s wife to find meaning in her husband’s ministry?
elements.answer1:
It is not wrong to find joy and meaning in supporting your husband’s calling. Marriage is a partnership, and sharing in ministry can be a genuine source of purpose and delight.

The danger is when that becomes the foundation of your identity — so that when the ministry goes through a hard season, or your husband faces criticism, or a church plant fails to grow, you feel as though you have failed, or as though your worth is in question.

Your identity in Christ is not borrowed from your husband. You are not “chosen in him” because you married a pastor. You are chosen in Christ because God set his love on you — personally, before the world was made (Ephesians 1:4). That identity cannot be taken from you by a difficult deacons’ meeting, an ungrateful congregation, or a season of ministry disappointment.

The ministry may change. Your husband’s role may change. Your identity in Christ does not.

elements.text1: Part 4 — The Role That Was Never Specified

Here is a fact that may surprise you: the Bible never describes the role of a pastor’s wife.

The New Testament has a great deal to say about pastors and elders — their character, their family life, their teaching responsibilities. It has a great deal to say about women in the church — their dignity, their gifts, their ministry. But it nowhere defines “pastor’s wife” as a church office, a specific role, or a set of required duties.

That means that every list of expectations placed on you — written or unwritten, spoken or assumed — comes from somewhere other than Scripture. It may come from culture, from tradition, from previous models of ministry, from your mother-in-law, or from your own imagination of what the role should look like. But it does not come directly from God.

This is not a licence to disengage from church life. It is an invitation to engage on a different basis — from identity, not from duty; from love, not from fear; from freedom, not from performance.

elements.question1: Make a list (mentally or on paper) of the expectations you feel are placed on you as a pastor’s wife. Where do you think each of those expectations comes from — Scripture, culture, tradition, your husband, the congregation, or yourself?
If there is no single biblical job description for a pastor’s wife, what does the Bible give you to build your life and ministry on? (You may want to look back at the passages in this session.)

deeper.question1: Is there an expectation placed on you that you have accepted without question, but which — on reflection — may not actually be from God? What would it take to lay that expectation down?

elements.term1: “But our family has always done it this way.”
elements.question1: What do you do when the expectations placed on you as a pastor’s wife come not from the church but from your own family — or your husband’s family?
elements.answer1:
In many Nigerian communities, the extended family has a strong voice in how a household is run — and a pastor’s household is no exception. In-laws may have firm ideas about what a good wife does, what she wears, how she spends her time, whether she works outside the home, how quickly she has children, and how involved she is in her husband’s ministry.

When those expectations are wise and loving, they are a gift. But when they conflict with your calling, your gifts, or your wellbeing, they deserve to be weighed carefully — not simply accepted because they are tradition.

The Bible calls a man to “leave his father and mother and be joined to his wife” (Genesis 2:24). That verse is about much more than geography. It describes a primary loyalty. A husband and wife together seek God’s will for their household — and that is not a decision made by committee.

This does not mean cutting off family relationships. It means that God’s call on your life, and your husband’s life, takes first place. Family expectations, however sincerely held, are secondary to that.

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements1: I have a clear sense of my own identity in Christ, separate from my role as a pastor’s wife.
I often feel pressure to meet expectations that I’m not sure are actually from God.
I feel free to be honest about my struggles with God, and with trusted people around me.
I believe that God values me personally, not just because of my husband’s ministry.
I regularly spend time with Jesus for my own sake, not just in preparation for ministry tasks.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: Ngozi, in our opening story, had lost herself in the role. Is there any part of her story that resonates with your own experience? What is God saying to you through it?
Jesus called Mary’s choice — sitting at his feet — “the one thing that is needed.” What would it mean, practically, for you to protect that “one thing” in the week ahead?
You are chosen, adopted, and accepted in Christ — before you ever did a single thing for the church. How does that truth change — or challenge — the way you think about yourself this week?

elements.text1:
You did not become valuable when you became a pastor’s wife. You were already known, already chosen, already loved — before the congregation existed, before your husband was called, before you said yes to any of it.

The role is real. The responsibilities matter. The ministry is a high and beautiful calling. But none of that is the foundation of who you are. The foundation is this: you are a daughter of God, chosen in Christ before the foundation of the world, fully accepted by the one whose opinion matters most.

Everything else — the serving, the leading, the giving, the enduring — flows from that. Or it should. That is what the rest of these sessions are about.

elements.question1: What is your challenge for this week?
elements.answer1:
Set aside twenty minutes this week — not to prepare anything, not to pray for the church, not to plan a meeting. Just to sit with Jesus, as Mary did.

You might read slowly through Psalm 139 — a psalm about being known by God completely and loved without reserve. Let it be a conversation rather than a task. Notice what stirs in you as you read.

Then write down, somewhere you will see it, one sentence that begins: “In Christ, I am…” — not what you do, but who you are.

Bring that sentence to the start of Session 2.

elements.question1: It’s worth having these passages open as you work through this session.

elements.text1:
At the end of Session 1, you were asked to write one sentence: “In Christ, I am…”

If you did it, you may have found it harder than expected. Many women find it surprisingly difficult to complete that sentence without slipping back into what they do — “I am a helper,” “I am a servant,” “I am a minister’s wife” — rather than who they are.

That difficulty is worth paying attention to.

Adaeze had been a believer for over fifteen years. She could preach on the love of God with conviction. She had led women’s retreats on God’s grace. She could quote John 3:16 and Romans 8:38–39 from memory. But one evening, after a particularly wounding remark from an older woman in the congregation — “You don’t really have the gift of hospitality, do you?” — she found herself weeping in her bedroom, wondering whether God was as disappointed in her as that woman seemed to be.

The truth she knew in her head had not yet reached the deepest part of her heart.

That is what this session is about. Not just knowing about God’s love, but being known by it — personally, specifically, all the way down.
Part 1 — Searched and Known

The application challenge from Session 1 pointed you to Psalm 139. We begin there now — because before we can receive love well, we need to grasp what it means to be truly known.

Many of us have a complicated relationship with being known. Being known means being seen — and being seen can feel dangerous when we are not sure we will be accepted for what is found. We manage our image. We present our best side. In ministry contexts especially, we learn to project competence and composure.

The psalmist has no such option. And he discovers that is not a threat — it is a relief.

elements.passageText1:
1O Lord, you examine me and know me.

2You know when I sit down and when I get up; even from far away you understand my motives.

3You carefully observe me when I travel or when I lie down to rest; you are aware of everything I do.

4Certainly my tongue does not frame a word without you, O Lord, being thoroughly aware of it.

5You squeeze me in from behind and in front; you place your hand on me.

6Your knowledge is beyond my comprehension; it is so far beyond me, I am unable to fathom it.

7Where can I go to escape your Spirit? Where can I flee to escape your presence?

8If I were to ascend to heaven, you would be there. If I were to sprawl out in Sheol, there you would be.

9If I were to fly away on the wings of the dawn, and settle down on the other side of the sea,

10even there your hand would guide me, your right hand would grab hold of me.

11If I were to say, “Certainly the darkness will cover me, and the light will turn to night all around me,”

12even the darkness is not too dark for you to see, and the night is as bright as day; darkness and light are the same to you.

13Certainly you made my mind and heart; you wove me together in my mother’s womb.

14I will give you thanks because your deeds are awesome and amazing. You knew me thoroughly;

15my bones were not hidden from you, when I was made in secret and sewed together in the depths of the earth.

16Your eyes saw me when I was inside the womb. All the days ordained for me were recorded in your scroll before one of them came into existence.

17How difficult it is for me to fathom your thoughts about me, O God! How vast is their sum total.

18If I tried to count them, they would outnumber the grains of sand. Even if I finished counting them, I would still have to contend with you.

elements.header1: “You have searched me and known me” (v1)
elements.question1: The psalmist describes God knowing his sitting, his rising, his thoughts from afar, every word before it reaches his tongue (vv1-4). This is complete, exhaustive knowledge. Does that feel comforting or frightening to you — and why?

deeper.question1: Is there a part of your life — a struggle, a doubt, a feeling — that you find it hard to believe God already knows? What would it mean if he knows it completely and loves you still?

elements.question1: In verses 7-12, the psalmist realises there is nowhere he can go to escape God’s presence. He considers darkness — and discovers that even darkness is not dark to God. How does this change the way you think about the parts of your life, or your heart, that feel hidden?

Verses 13-16 move to the wonder of being made: “I am fearfully and wonderfully made” (v14). This is not flattery — it is theology. God made you specifically, deliberately, attentively. What does it do to your sense of self-worth to know that you were not an accident — that you were crafted?

deeper.question1: Verse 16 says God saw your unformed substance and that all your days were written in his book before one of them came to be. That includes the days you are living now — in ministry, in marriage, in difficulty. How does that change the way you interpret your current season?

elements.question1: The psalm ends (vv17-18) not with relief or triumphant declaration, but with quiet wonder: “How vast the sum of them!” The psalmist is simply overwhelmed by the extent of God’s attention to him. When did you last feel something like that wonder — a sense of being attended to by God?

elements.text1: Part 2 — The Woman at the Well

Psalm 139 gives us the theological foundation: God knows us completely. The Gospels show us what that looks like when it walks into a specific person’s life.

The account of Jesus and the woman at the well is one of the longest and most detailed one-on-one conversations Jesus has with anyone in the Gospels. His conversation partner is a Samaritan woman — someone who would have been excluded from the religious life of her day on three counts: her ethnicity, her gender, and her personal history.

Jesus chose her. He sought her out. He spoke to her first.

elements.passageText1:
4But he had to pass through Samaria. 5Now he came to a Samaritan town called Sychar, near the plot of land that Jacob had given to his son Joseph. 6Jacob’s well was there, so Jesus, since he was tired from the journey, sat right down beside the well. It was about noon.

7A Samaritan woman came to draw water. Jesus said to her, “Give me some water to drink.” 8(For his disciples had gone off into the town to buy supplies.) 9So the Samaritan woman said to him, “How can you—a Jew—ask me, a Samaritan woman, for water to drink?” (For Jews use nothing in common with Samaritans.)

10Jesus answered her, “If you had known the gift of God and who it is who said to you, ‘Give me some water to drink,’ you would have asked him, and he would have given you living water.” 11“Sir,” the woman said to him, “you have no bucket and the well is deep; where then do you get this living water? 12Surely you’re not greater than our ancestor Jacob, are you? For he gave us this well and drank from it himself, along with his sons and his livestock.”

13Jesus replied, “Everyone who drinks some of this water will be thirsty again. 14But whoever drinks some of the water that I will give him will never be thirsty again, but the water that I will give him will become in him a fountain of water springing up to eternal life.” 15The woman said to him, “Sir, give me this water, so that I will not be thirsty or have to come here to draw water.” 16He said to her, “Go call your husband and come back here.” 17The woman replied, “I have no husband.” Jesus said to her, “Right you are when you said, ‘I have no husband,’ 18for you have had five husbands, and the man you are living with now is not your husband. This you said truthfully!”

19The woman said to him, “Sir, I see that you are a prophet. 20Our fathers worshiped on this mountain, and you people say that the place where people must worship is in Jerusalem.” 21Jesus said to her, “Believe me, woman, a time is coming when you will worship the Father neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem. 22You people worship what you do not know. We worship what we know, because salvation is from the Jews. 23But a time is coming—and now is here—when the true worshipers will worship the Father in spirit and truth, for the Father seeks such people to be his worshipers. 24God is spirit, and the people who worship him must worship in spirit and truth.” 25The woman said to him, “I know that Messiah is coming” (the one called Christ); “whenever he comes, he will tell us everything.” 26Jesus said to her, “I, the one speaking to you, am he.”

27Now at that very moment his disciples came back. They were shocked because he was speaking with a woman. However, no one said, “What do you want?” or “Why are you speaking with her?” 28Then the woman left her water jar, went off into the town and said to the people, 29“Come, see a man who told me everything I ever did. Surely he can’t be the Messiah, can he?” 30So they left the town and began coming to him.

elements.question1: Most Jewish travellers avoided Samaria entirely, taking a longer route. The text says Jesus “had to” go through it — as if driven by purpose. He arrives at a well at noon, alone, and waits. He is waiting for her. What does this deliberate seeking-out tell you about how Jesus relates to people who feel on the margins?

Jesus knows the woman’s full history (v18) — five husbands, and the man she is with now. He does not use that knowledge to shame her. He uses it to show her that he sees her — really sees her. She becomes the first person in John’s Gospel to whom Jesus directly reveals that he is the Messiah (v26). Why do you think he chose her for that revelation?

deeper.question1: The woman initially tries to deflect Jesus’ personal knowledge into a theological debate about worship (vv19-20) — a common response when a conversation gets uncomfortably close. Do you ever do something similar — retreating into theological discussion or ministry activity when God’s word starts to feel personal? What might he be pressing on?

elements.question1: After meeting Jesus, the woman goes back to her town and says: “Come and see a man who told me everything I ever did” (v29). Being fully known by Jesus became her testimony — not something to hide, but something to share. What would it mean for your own witness if being known by God became a source of confidence rather than anxiety?

elements.term1: “I have to keep up appearances for the congregation.”
elements.question1: Is there pressure in your context to present a version of yourself that is more put-together than the reality? What does that cost you?
elements.answer1:
There is a real and legitimate sense in which a pastor and his family set a tone for a congregation. Character matters. Conduct matters. But there is a significant difference between integrity — living consistently and honestly — and performance — projecting an image that does not match reality.

When a pastor’s wife feels she must hide her marriage difficulties, her doubts, her grief, or her struggles with sin in order to protect the ministry’s reputation, something has gone wrong. The congregation is not being led — it is being deceived. And the pastor’s wife is being slowly hollowed out.

The gospel does not require image management. It requires honesty before God and, in appropriate ways, before one another. Paul wrote to the Corinthians out of “great distress and anguish of heart and with many tears” (2 Corinthians 2:4). He did not hide his struggles from the churches he served. He brought them into the light.

The woman at the well was known — completely — and became a witness. Not in spite of being known, but because of it.

elements.text1: Part 3 — Loved, Not Because of What You Offer

There is another woman in Luke’s Gospel who encounters Jesus — and her story approaches the question of love from a completely different angle. She does not have a long conversation with him. She does not become a witness or a teacher. She simply weeps at his feet, and he defends her.

That, too, is a picture of what it means to be loved by God.

elements.passageText1: 36Now one of the Pharisees asked Jesus to have dinner with him, so he went into the Pharisee’s house and took his place at the table. 37Then when a woman of that town, who was a sinner, learned that Jesus was dining at the Pharisee’s house, she brought an alabaster jar of perfumed oil. 38As she stood behind him at his feet, weeping, she began to wet his feet with her tears. She wiped them with her hair, kissed them, and anointed them with the perfumed oil. 39Now when the Pharisee who had invited him saw this, he said to himself, “If this man were a prophet, he would know who and what kind of woman this is who is touching him, that she is a sinner.” 40So Jesus answered him, “Simon, I have something to say to you.” He replied, “Say it, Teacher.” 41“A certain creditor had two debtors; one owed him 500 silver coins, and the other fifty. 42When they could not pay, he canceled the debts of both. Now which of them will love him more?” 43Simon answered, “I suppose the one who had the bigger debt canceled.” Jesus said to him, “You have judged rightly.” 44Then, turning toward the woman, he said to Simon, “Do you see this woman? I entered your house. You gave me no water for my feet, but she has wet my feet with her tears and wiped them with her hair. 45You gave me no kiss of greeting, but from the time I entered she has not stopped kissing my feet. 46You did not anoint my head with oil, but she has anointed my feet with perfumed oil. 47Therefore I tell you, her sins, which were many, are forgiven, thus she loved much; but the one who is forgiven little loves little.” 48Then Jesus said to her, “Your sins are forgiven.” 49But those who were at the table with him began to say among themselves, “Who is this, who even forgives sins?” 50He said to the woman, “Your faith has saved you; go in peace.”

elements.question1: Simon the Pharisee invited Jesus to dinner — an act of social respectability. The woman who enters is described only as “a sinner” — a term that in this context likely means she was known in the town as morally disreputable. Simon sees her intrusion as an embarrassment. Jesus sees her act of love as beautiful. What does the contrast between Simon and Jesus tell you about the difference between religious propriety and genuine love?

Jesus tells a small parable (vv41-43) about two debtors — one who owed a little, one who owed a great deal, both forgiven. He concludes: “The one who is forgiven little loves little.” He is not saying Simon has less to be forgiven. He is saying Simon thinks he has less to be forgiven. What is the connection between a deep sense of being forgiven and a deep experience of love for God?

deeper.question1: Ministry can sometimes create a subtle spiritual pride — a sense that we are on the giving rather than the receiving end of grace. Is there a way in which your ministry role has made it harder, rather than easier, to see yourself as someone who needs great forgiveness?

elements.question1: Jesus says to the woman: “Your faith has saved you. Go in peace” (v50). She came with nothing to offer except her tears and her alabaster jar. She left forgiven, at peace, and publicly honoured by Jesus in a room full of people who looked down on her. What does this say about the basis on which God receives people — and receives you?

elements.text1: Part 4 — Nothing Can Separate You

The three passages we have read so far — Psalm 139, John 4, Luke 7 — all approach the same reality from different angles: God’s knowledge of you is complete, his pursuit of you is deliberate, and his love for you is not conditional on your performance or your reputation.

Paul, in Romans 8, brings this to its fullest and most sweeping expression. After eleven chapters of careful theological argument about the human condition and God’s rescue, he pauses to ask a series of rhetorical questions — and to answer them with something approaching amazed disbelief that the answer is as good as it is.

elements.passageText1: 31What then shall we say about these things? If God is for us, who can be against us? 32Indeed, he who did not spare his own Son, but gave him up for us all—how will he not also, along with him, freely give us all things? 33Who will bring any charge against God’s elect? It is God who justifies. 34Who is the one who will condemn? Christ is the one who died (and more than that, he was raised), who is at the right hand of God, and who also is interceding for us. 35Who will separate us from the love of Christ? Will trouble, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or danger, or sword? 36As it is written, “For your sake we encounter death all day long; we were considered as sheep to be slaughtered.” 37No, in all these things we have complete victory through him who loved us! 38For I am convinced that neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor heavenly rulers, nor things that are present, nor things to come, nor powers, 39nor height, nor depth, nor anything else in creation will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord.

elements.header1: “Who can bring any charge against God’s elect?” (v33)
elements.question1: Paul lists a series of voices that might try to bring a charge against us — accusers, condemners, things present and things to come. In your own life, which of these feels most real? Whose voice most often whispers that you are not enough, not worthy, not acceptable?

deeper.question1: Paul’s answer to every accuser is not a defence of his own record — it is a statement about what God has done: “It is God who justifies” (v33), “Christ is the one who died” (v34). Why is pointing to Christ’s record rather than your own the only answer that actually holds up?

elements.question1: Paul asks: “Who will separate us from the love of Christ?” and then lists a catalogue of harsh realities: trouble, distress, persecution, famine, nakedness, danger, sword (v35). These are not hypothetical — Paul experienced most of them. Ministry life in Nigeria can involve real versions of many of these. Which of them feels closest to your own experience — and how does Paul’s answer speak into that?

Paul’s final declaration is astonishing in its scope: nothing — not death, not life, not angels, not rulers, not present circumstances, not what is coming — nothing “will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord” (vv38-39). What would it look like for you to live from that security this week — in your marriage, in your ministry, in your home?

elements.term1: Learning to receive love, not just give it
elements.question1: Why do people in ministry sometimes find it hard to receive care, rather than always being the one who gives it?
elements.answer1:
People in ministry — pastors, their wives, church workers — often develop a strong identity around giving. They visit the sick, counsel the struggling, pray for the grieving. Over time, this can make it genuinely difficult to be on the receiving end. Receiving help can feel like weakness. Receiving care can feel uncomfortable, even inappropriate for someone in a leadership role.

But the ability to receive love — from God and from other people — is not a weakness. It is a spiritual discipline. Mary of Bethany sat and received from Jesus while Martha served. Jesus received the ministrations of the woman with the alabaster jar. Paul received financial support from the Philippian church with gratitude rather than embarrassment (Philippians 4:15–16).

If you find it hard to let people care for you, it is worth asking why. Sometimes it is pride. Sometimes it is a fear of being seen as needy. Sometimes it is simply a role-identity that has become too rigid. Whatever the reason, learning to receive well is part of being known and loved — and part of what makes genuine community possible.

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements1: I genuinely believe that God knows me completely and loves me still.
I find it easy to receive care and love from others, not just to give it.
I am able to be honest about my struggles with at least one trusted person.
The voices of criticism or accusation feel louder to me than the voice of God’s love.
I experience God’s presence as personal — as someone who knows and sees me specifically.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: Adaeze, in our opening story, knew the truth of God’s love in her head but felt the wound of criticism in her heart. Is there a gap like that in your own life — between what you know theologically and what you feel in your deepest moments? What do you think that gap is about?
Of the four passages in this session — Psalm 139, John 4, Luke 7, Romans 8 — which one landed most personally for you, and why?
What is one specific thing you want to do differently this week, in response to what you have seen about being known and loved by God?

elements.text1:
You are not loved because you are useful. You are not known because you are impressive. You are not accepted because you have held it together.

God searched you and knew you before you were formed. Jesus sought you out and named you — like Mary in the garden, like the woman at the well, like the woman at Simon’s table. And nothing — not failure, not criticism, not a difficult season of ministry, not a wound from within the church — nothing can undo what God has declared over you in Christ.

The role will make demands on you. The congregation will have opinions. But the one who made you, knows you, and loves you has already said everything that matters.

You are his. Fully. Finally. Forever.

elements.question1: What is your challenge for this week?
elements.answer1:
Choose one of the four passages from this session and read it every day this week — not to prepare anything, not to teach it, but simply to let it speak to you.

As you read, try to receive it personally. Put your name into the text. Psalm 139:1 becomes: “O Lord, you have searched Adaeze and known her.” Romans 8:38 becomes: “I am convinced that nothing can separate Adaeze from the love of God.”

This is not a technique — it is a practice of letting the truth move from your head to your heart. Many women find that reading the same passage repeatedly across a week does something that reading many passages once cannot.

Bring one sentence from your reading to share at the start of Session 3.

elements.question3: It’s worth having these passages open as you work through this session.

elements.text3:
Blessing had been married to Pastor Emeka for eight years. She loved him. She respected his calling. She prayed for him daily, covered for him when he was exhausted, and had learned to read the signs that told her when he needed space and when he needed someone to talk to.

But there were things she never said. Not because he had forbidden it — he had not — but because she had learned, gradually and without quite noticing, to make herself smaller. If she had an opinion about the direction of the church, she kept it to herself. If she was struggling, she waited until he had time — which was rarely. If she disagreed with a decision he had made, she would mention it once, gently, and then let it go.

She told herself this was submission. She told herself a good pastor’s wife makes her husband’s ministry easier, not harder.

What she had not yet asked was whether this was what God actually intended — or whether she and Emeka had both quietly settled for something less than a marriage.

This session is about marriage. Not marriage as a role to perform, but marriage as a relationship God designed — with a specific shape, a specific purpose, and a depth that ministry can either deepen or quietly hollow out, depending on how you tend it.
Part 1 — What Marriage Was Made For

Before we can understand what a pastor’s marriage should look like, we need to go back to where marriage begins — not in church tradition, not in cultural custom, but in the opening chapters of Genesis. What did God have in mind when he made the first marriage?

elements.passageText1:
18The Lord God said, “It is not good for the man to be alone. I will make a companion for him who corresponds to him.” 19The Lord God formed out of the ground every living animal of the field and every bird of the air. He brought them to the man to see what he would name them, and whatever the man called each living creature, that was its name. 20So the man named all the animals, the birds of the air, and the living creatures of the field, but for Adam no companion who corresponded to him was found. 21So the Lord God caused the man to fall into a deep sleep, and while he was asleep, he took part of the man’s side and closed up the place with flesh. 22Then the Lord God made a woman from the part he had taken out of the man, and he brought her to the man. 23Then the man said, “This one at last is bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh; this one will be called ‘woman,’ for she was taken out of man.”

24That is why a man leaves his father and mother and unites with his wife, and they become one family. 25The man and his wife were both naked, but they were not ashamed.

elements.header3: "It is not good for the man to be alone" (v18)
elements.question3: Before sin entered the world — before the Fall, before difficulty — God looked at the man and said something was not good. Solitude was not enough, even in a perfect world. What does this tell you about the kind of relationship God built marriage to be — and how central companionship is to his design?

deeper.question3: In ministry, pastors can be surrounded by people all week while being deeply lonely at home — and so can their wives. In what ways might a busy ministry actually increase isolation within a marriage, rather than reducing it? What would it take to be genuinely not alone together?

elements.question3: The word translated "helper" in verse 18 (ezer in Hebrew) is the same word used elsewhere in the Old Testament to describe God himself as Israel’s helper (Psalm 121:2, Psalm 124:8). It is a word of strength, not subordination. How does knowing that change the way you think about the calling to be a helper to your husband?

Verse 24 describes a man leaving his father and mother and being joined to his wife — the two becoming "one flesh." This is a complete union: physical, emotional, spiritual. In what ways does ministry life put pressure on that union? What gets squeezed out first?

deeper.question3: Verse 25 notes that the man and his wife were both naked and felt no shame. This is a picture of complete transparency — nothing hidden, nothing managed. How close does your marriage come to that kind of openness? What would need to change to move towards it?

elements.text3: Part 2 — Submission, Love, and What Both Actually Mean

Ephesians 5 is perhaps the most discussed — and most misused — passage about marriage in the New Testament. It contains the word submission, which in some contexts has been used to justify silence, passivity, and even the endurance of harm. It deserves careful reading.

What is often missed is that Paul’s instruction to wives comes in the middle of a much larger instruction to all believers: "Submit to one another out of reverence for Christ" (v21). Mutual submission is the foundation. What follows is not a hierarchy of power but a description of how love expresses itself differently for a husband and a wife — and the standard Paul sets for husbands is extraordinarily demanding.

elements.passageText1: 22Wives, submit to your husbands as to the Lord, 23because the husband is the head of the wife as also Christ is the head of the church (he himself being the savior of the body). 24But as the church submits to Christ, so also wives should submit to their husbands in everything. 25Husbands, love your wives just as Christ loved the church and gave himself for her 26to sanctify her by cleansing her with the washing of the water by the word, 27so that he may present the church to himself as glorious—not having a stain or wrinkle, or any such blemish, but holy and blameless. 28In the same way husbands ought to love their wives as their own bodies. He who loves his wife loves himself. 29For no one has ever hated his own body, but he feeds it and takes care of it, just as Christ also does the church, 30because we are members of his body. 31For this reason a man will leave his father and mother and will be joined to his wife, and the two will become one flesh. 32This mystery is great—but I am actually speaking with reference to Christ and the church. 33Nevertheless, each one of you must also love his own wife as he loves himself, and the wife must respect her husband.

elements.question3: Paul says wives should submit to their husbands "as to the Lord." He is not saying that a husband is the Lord, or that obedience to a husband is the same as obedience to God. He is describing the quality of the orientation — willing, trusting, not grudging. What is the difference between submission as Paul describes it here, and the kind of diminishment Blessing experienced in our opening story?

deeper.question3: Submission "as to the Lord" presupposes that the Lord is trustworthy, good, and acting in your best interests. When a husband does not reflect those qualities, what does that do to the foundation of this instruction? How should a wife navigate submission when her husband’s leadership is absent, harmful, or unclear?

elements.question3: Paul’s instruction to husbands (vv25-30) is far longer and far more demanding than his instruction to wives. He calls husbands to love their wives as Christ loved the church — a love that was self-giving, sacrificial, and costly to the point of death. How does this vision of a husband’s love change the way you think about the context in which a wife’s submission makes sense?

Paul says a husband should love his wife as he loves "his own body" (v28) — nourishing and cherishing her. What would it look like for a pastor to apply that standard to how he treats his wife, specifically in the context of ministry — protecting her time, her rest, her voice, her wellbeing?

deeper.question3: This study is written for wives, not for husbands. But you know your marriage from the inside. Without moving into criticism of your husband, is there a way in which you could gently, lovingly raise one aspect of this passage with him — not as an accusation, but as an invitation to reflect together on what God’s design for your marriage looks like?

elements.term3: "A pastor’s wife must always agree with her husband in public."
elements.question3: Is it disloyal — or even unspiritual — for a pastor’s wife to disagree with her husband?
elements.answer3:
Loyalty and honesty are not the same thing — and a good marriage requires both.

A wife who agrees with everything her husband says and never raises a concern is not being a faithful partner. She is being a mirror. And a mirror cannot help a man see his blind spots, course-correct before a mistake becomes a crisis, or grow into a wiser leader.

Proverbs 27:17 says: "Iron sharpens iron, and one person sharpens another." That picture assumes friction. Sharpening does not happen when two soft surfaces touch.

In many traditional Nigerian contexts, there is a strong expectation that a pastor’s wife will be visibly supportive and publicly agreeable — that any sign of disagreement, even in private, would dishonour her husband or undermine his authority. This expectation is worth examining carefully.

The biblical picture of a good wife — including in Proverbs 31 — is not one of silent compliance. It is one of active wisdom, capable judgment, and shared responsibility. Her husband trusts her precisely because she brings something real to the partnership.

Disagreement, raised with respect and love, in the right time and place, is an act of faithfulness — to your husband, and to God.

elements.text3: Part 3 — A Different Kind of Partnership

1 Peter 3 addresses wives in a specific and difficult situation — some of the women Peter was writing to were married to unbelieving husbands, men who were not following Christ. His counsel is gentle and dignified. But even in this passage, written for a situation harder than most of us face, the picture that emerges is of a woman with genuine inner strength, not one who has been reduced to invisibility.

elements.passageText1: 1In the same way, wives, be subject to your own husbands. Then, even if some are disobedient to the word, they will be won over without a word by the way you live, 2when they see your pure and reverent conduct. 3Let your beauty not be external—the braiding of hair and wearing of gold jewelry or fine clothes— 4but the inner person of the heart, the lasting beauty of a gentle and tranquil spirit, which is precious in God’s sight. 5For in the same way the holy women who hoped in God long ago adorned themselves by being subject to their husbands, 6like Sarah who obeyed Abraham, calling him lord. You become her children when you do what is good and have no fear in doing so. 7Husbands, in the same way, treat your wives with consideration as the weaker partners and show them honor as fellow heirs of the grace of life. In this way nothing will hinder your prayers.

elements.header3: "The imperishable quality of a gentle and quiet spirit" (v4)
elements.question3: Peter commends a "gentle and quiet spirit" — but the Greek word for quiet (hesychios) refers to a settled, untroubled disposition, not to silence. It is the opposite of anxiety, not the opposite of speech. How does that distinction change your understanding of what Peter is calling women to?

Peter holds up Sarah as an example — a woman who called Abraham "lord." But Sarah also laughed at God’s promise (Genesis 18:12), argued with Abraham (Genesis 16:5-6), and told him firmly to send Hagar away (Genesis 21:10) — and God told Abraham to listen to her (Genesis 21:12). What kind of woman does the full picture of Sarah suggest — and how does that complicate simple readings of "submission"?

deeper.question3: Verse 7 turns to husbands with a striking phrase: they are to treat their wives with understanding and honour, as "fellow heirs of the grace of life." Equal heirs. That language places husband and wife on exactly the same footing before God. How often does that equality feel real in your marriage — and what would it look like to live from it more fully?

elements.text3: Part 4 — Partner, Not Assistant

The final passage for this session has been used in many ways — sometimes to celebrate women, sometimes to burden them, sometimes both at once. Proverbs 31 describes a woman of strong character, wide capability, and genuine influence. She is active in commerce, generous to the poor, wise in counsel, and trusted completely by her husband.

She is not his assistant. She is his partner — with a sphere of her own, strengths of her own, and a reputation that stands independently alongside his.

elements.passageText1: 10Who can find a wife of noble character? For her value is far more than rubies. 11Her husband’s heart has trusted her, and he does not lack the dividends. 12She has rewarded him with good and not harm all the days of her life. 13She sought out wool and flax, then worked happily with her hands. 14She was like the merchant ships; she would bring in her food from afar. 15Then she rose while it was still night, and provided food for her household and a portion to her female servants. 16She considered a field and bought it; from her own income she planted a vineyard. 17She clothed herself in might, and she strengthened her arms. 18She perceived that her merchandise was good. Her lamp would not go out in the night. 19She extended her hands to the spool, and her hands grasped the spindle. 20She opened her hand to the poor, and extended her hands to the needy. 21She would not fear for her household in winter, because all her household were clothed with scarlet, 22because she had made coverings for herself; and because her clothing was fine linen and purple. 23Her husband is well-known in the city gate when he sits with the elders of the land. 24She made linen garments then sold them, and traded belts to the merchants; 25her clothing was strong and splendid; and she laughed at the time to come. 26She has opened her mouth with wisdom, with loving instruction on her tongue. 27Watching over the ways of her household, she would not eat the bread of idleness. 28Her children have risen and called her blessed; her husband also has praised her: 29“Many daughters have done valiantly, but you have surpassed them all!” 30Charm is deceitful and beauty is fleeting. A woman who fears the Lord—she makes herself praiseworthy. 31Give her credit for what she has accomplished, and let her works praise her in the city gates.

elements.question3: The passage begins with trust — not obedience, not compliance, but complete trust. This implies that she is someone worth trusting: someone with good judgment, reliable character, and proven wisdom. What is the difference between a husband who controls his wife and a husband who trusts her — and what does each approach produce in a marriage over time?

The Proverbs 31 woman is energetic in business (vv13-18), generous in charity (v20), wise in speech (v26), and attentive to her household (vv21-22, 27). She is not doing all of this instead of her husband — he is "known at the city gate" (v23), a respected public figure in his own right. They each have a sphere. What does a marriage look like when both partners are flourishing in their respective callings — rather than one diminishing so the other can grow?

deeper.question3: Verse 31 says her works praise her "in the city gates" — in public. Her reputation is her own, earned by her own character and effort. As a pastor’s wife, do you have a sense of your own calling and contribution that is distinct from your husband’s ministry — or has his role absorbed yours? What would it mean to recover or develop that?

elements.question3: Verse 30 is often quoted: "A woman who fears the Lord is to be praised." This, not her productivity or her domestic skill, is the root of everything else described. What does it mean for the fear of the Lord to be the source of a woman’s identity — and how does that differ from the fear of the congregation’s opinion, or the fear of failing in the role?

elements.term3: "My husband’s calling is more important than mine."
elements.question3: Is it true that a pastor’s calling takes priority over his wife’s gifts and sense of calling?
elements.answer3:
God calls individuals — not titles, and not couples as a single unit with one calling between them. When a man is called to pastoral ministry, that call is on him. It does not automatically define what his wife is called to do, what gifts she should exercise, or how she should spend her time and energy.

In practice, a couple must make real decisions together about how to steward two lives, two sets of gifts, and one household. Those decisions involve genuine sacrifice and negotiation on both sides. But sacrifice chosen freely, in love, is very different from sacrifice assumed automatically — where the wife’s gifts, ambitions, and sense of calling are treated as background noise to the main event of her husband’s ministry.

God placed gifts in you before your husband was called to ministry. Those gifts did not become less significant when you married. They are still his gift to you and, through you, to others.

This does not mean a pastor’s wife should not support her husband’s ministry — of course she should, as he should support hers. But support flows in both directions in a healthy marriage. A husband who is secure in his calling will not feel threatened by a wife who has a calling of her own.

elements.term3: "In our culture, a wife does not challenge her husband."
elements.question3: What do you do when cultural expectations about a wife’s role conflict with what the Bible actually teaches about marriage?
elements.answer3:
In many Nigerian communities — across ethnic groups and religious traditions — a wife is expected to defer to her husband in all things, to speak only when invited to, and to express disagreement only indirectly, if at all. These expectations are often deeply felt and socially enforced. They may come from family, from community elders, from the wider church culture, or from patterns modelled across generations.

It is important to treat these expectations with respect — they are not always wrong, and they often carry genuine wisdom about the value of honour, loyalty, and restraint. But cultural tradition is not the same as biblical teaching, and the two do not always agree.

The Bible’s vision of marriage is one of mutual love, mutual service, and genuine partnership between two people who are equally heirs of God’s grace (1 Peter 3:7). That vision may be in tension with certain cultural patterns — and when it is, the Bible’s vision takes priority.

This does not mean acting in ways that are unnecessarily provocative or that bring genuine harm to a family relationship. Wisdom matters. Timing matters. But it does mean that a pastor’s wife has both the right and the responsibility — before God — to bring her whole self to her marriage: her voice, her wisdom, her concerns, and her gifts.

She is not a lesser partner. She is a fellow heir.

elements.instruction3: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements3: I feel free to express my opinions and concerns honestly within my marriage.
I believe my husband and I are genuine partners, not employer and employee.
I have a sense of my own calling that is distinct from my husband’s ministry.
I feel that my husband honours and values my contribution to our marriage and family.
Ministry pressures have not significantly damaged the intimacy and friendship in our marriage.

elements.text3: Personal Reflection

elements.question3: Blessing, in our opening story, had learned to make herself smaller — telling herself it was submission. Looking at the passages in this session, do you think she was right? And is there any way in which you have done something similar?

Of the images of marriage in this session — the ezer who is a strong helper, the mutually submitting partners of Ephesians 5, the fellow heirs of 1 Peter 3, the trusted and capable woman of Proverbs 31 — which image do you most need to hold onto right now, and why?

What is one honest, loving conversation you could have with your husband in the coming week — not a confrontation, but a genuine sharing — about your marriage, your partnership, or your sense of calling?

elements.text3:
Marriage in ministry is both a gift and a pressure. It can be the place where you are most known, most supported, and most yourself — or it can become one more performance, one more role to maintain, the closest relationship slowly hollowed out by busyness and unspoken needs.

God did not design it that way. He designed it for one flesh — for the kind of union where two people become genuinely more together than they were apart. Where a man’s calling is sharpened by a woman’s wisdom. Where a woman’s gifts are honoured and given room to grow. Where both partners are known, and neither is alone.

That marriage is worth fighting for — not against each other, but together, against everything that tries to pull you apart.

You are not your husband’s assistant. You are his partner, his equal before God, and — in the best and fullest sense — his ezer: a helper of strength, not of silence.

elements.question3: What is your challenge for this week?
elements.answer3:
Choose one thing this week that is just for your marriage — not for the ministry, not for the congregation, not for the children. Something that is only for you and your husband.

It does not need to be elaborate. A walk. A meal without phones. A conversation about something other than church business. An honest question: "How are you — really?" And the same question turned back towards you, with enough time to answer it properly.

If that kind of conversation feels awkward or unfamiliar, that itself is worth noticing. What has crowded it out? And what would you need to do differently to protect it?

Bring one reflection from your week to share at the start of Session 4.

chapters.chapterTitle3: Called, Not Appointed — Session 4: The Hidden Life

elements.question3: It’s worth having these passages open as you work through this session.

elements.text3:
Chisom had a reputation in the diocese. People said she was everything a pastor’s wife should be: well-dressed, well-spoken, gracious under pressure, always present, always available, always composed. Other pastors’ wives were sometimes pointed to her as an example.

What they did not see was Sunday evening. The weariness that came over her the moment the last person left. The irritability she directed at her children that she would never have shown to a church member. The way she scrolled her phone for an hour rather than pray, because she had nothing left. The resentment she had stopped examining because examining it felt too dangerous.

She was not a hypocrite in the calculated sense — she was not deliberately deceiving anyone. She had simply learned, over years, to maintain the surface so well that she had lost track of what was underneath it.

The hidden life — the life of the heart, the interior life that only God sees — is what this session is about. Not because the outward life doesn’t matter, but because the outward life eventually becomes whatever the hidden life actually is. You cannot maintain a gap between them indefinitely. And in ministry, the gap, when it collapses, tends to collapse in public.
Part 1 — What God Sees

The story of Samuel anointing a new king is one of the most direct statements in the Old Testament about the difference between what people see and what God sees. Samuel is a prophet — he hears from God, he has good judgment, he has long experience. And he still gets it wrong on his first seven attempts, because he is looking at the wrong thing.

elements.passageText1:
1The Lord said to Samuel, “How long do you intend to mourn for Saul? I have rejected him as king over Israel. Fill your horn with olive oil and go. I am sending you to Jesse in Bethlehem, for I have selected a king for myself from among his sons.”

2Samuel replied, “How can I go? Saul will hear about it and kill me!” But the Lord said, “Take a heifer with you and say, ‘I have come to sacrifice to the Lord.’ 3Then invite Jesse to the sacrifice, and I will show you what you should do. You will anoint for me the one I point out to you.”

4Samuel did what the Lord told him. When he arrived in Bethlehem, the elders of the city were afraid to meet him. They said, “Do you come in peace?” 5He replied, “Yes, in peace. I have come to sacrifice to the Lord. Consecrate yourselves and come with me to the sacrifice.” So he consecrated Jesse and his sons and invited them to the sacrifice.

6When they arrived, Samuel noticed Eliab and said to himself, “Surely, here before the Lord stands his chosen king.” 7But the Lord said to Samuel, “Don’t be impressed by his appearance or his height, for I have rejected him. God does not view things the way people do. People look on the outward appearance, but the Lord looks at the heart.”

8Then Jesse called Abinadab and presented him to Samuel. But Samuel said, “The Lord has not chosen this one either.” 9Then Jesse presented Shammah. But Samuel said, “The Lord has not chosen this one either.” 10Jesse presented seven of his sons to Samuel. But Samuel said to Jesse, “The Lord has not chosen any of these.” 11Then Samuel asked Jesse, “Is that all the young men?” Jesse replied, “There is still the youngest one, but he’s taking care of the flock.” Samuel said to Jesse, “Send and get him, for we cannot turn our attention to other things until he comes here.”

12So Jesse had him brought in. Now he was ruddy, with attractive eyes and a handsome appearance. The Lord said, “Go and anoint him. This is the one.” 13So Samuel took the horn full of olive oil and anointed him in the presence of his brothers. The Spirit of the Lord rushed upon David from that day onward. Then Samuel got up and went to Ramah.

elements.question3: Samuel sees Eliab — tall, impressive, probably the obvious choice — and assumes he must be the one. God corrects him directly: "I do not look at the things people look at." What are the things that people typically look at when assessing a pastor’s wife? And what do you think God looks at instead?

deeper.question3: God’s chosen king is out in a field looking after sheep — not at the gathering, not presenting himself, not competing for the role. He is simply being faithful in a small, unseen place. Is there something in your own life that you are tending faithfully but that no one notices or affirms? What does this passage say to you about the value of that hidden faithfulness?

elements.question3: David is described in verse 12 as having "beautiful eyes" and a "handsome appearance" — so it is not that outward appearance is irrelevant. But verse 7 makes clear it is not the primary thing. What do you think God means by the "heart"? What is he actually looking at?

Jesse did not even call David in from the fields when the prophet came — he had not considered him a serious candidate. The one God had chosen was the one his own father had overlooked. What does it say about God’s character that he repeatedly chooses the overlooked, the youngest, the one not in the room?

deeper.question3: Ministry contexts have their own hierarchies — senior pastors’ wives, denominational leaders’ wives, the well-known and well-connected. Have you ever felt overlooked in those hierarchies? How does this passage speak into that experience?

elements.text3: Part 2 — The Danger of the Cleaned-Up Outside

If 1 Samuel 16 shows us what God looks for, Matthew 23 shows us what he finds most troubling — and it is not immorality or theological error, but something subtler: the careful maintenance of an outward appearance that no longer matches the inward reality.

Jesus reserved his sharpest words for religious leaders who had mastered the exterior while neglecting the interior. His critique is not aimed at pagans or obvious sinners. It is aimed at people deeply embedded in religious life — people who prayed, fasted, gave, and served, but whose inner life had become, in his words, full of "hypocrisy and lawlessness."

This is a passage for people in ministry. It is uncomfortable precisely because it is so close to home.
