elements.question3: Jesus uses two images — a cup clean on the outside but dirty within, and a whitewashed tomb: beautiful on the surface, full of death underneath. These are images of a gap between appearance and reality. Where do you think that gap is most likely to open up in the life of a pastor’s wife — and what tends to cause it?

deeper.question3: Jesus says if you clean the inside, the outside will also be clean (v26). He is not saying the outside doesn’t matter — he is saying it follows from the inside, not the other way around. What would it mean for you to focus your spiritual energy on the interior rather than the exterior — and what would need to change?

elements.question3: The Pharisees Jesus is addressing were not lazy or casual about religion — they were extraordinarily disciplined. The problem was not lack of effort, but misdirected effort: energy spent on the visible rather than the real. Can you recognise that temptation in your own life — putting more effort into how things appear than into what is actually true?

elements.term3: "I have to hold it together for everyone else."
elements.question3: Is it part of a pastor’s wife’s calling to always appear strong and composed — for the sake of the congregation?
elements.answer3:
There is a version of this that is genuinely virtuous: choosing not to unload personal struggles on people who are already carrying heavy burdens; maintaining composure in moments of pastoral crisis when others need steadiness; protecting the congregation from unnecessary worry. Discretion and self-discipline are real gifts.

But there is another version that is quietly destructive: never allowing anyone to see beneath the surface; treating vulnerability as a threat to be managed rather than a need to be met; maintaining the appearance of strength long past the point where it is honest.

The Bible draws a consistent line between appropriate discretion and the kind of performance that becomes its own form of pride. Maintaining a composed exterior while the interior is in distress is not strength — it is a form of isolation, and it tends to produce exactly the brittleness it is designed to prevent.

Jesus did not hold it together for everyone else. He wept at Lazarus’s tomb. He was "troubled in spirit" before his arrest (John 13:21). He cried out from the cross. His composure, when he showed it, was real — rooted in genuine trust in the Father, not in an unwillingness to be seen.

The congregation does not need a pastor’s wife who is always composed. It needs one who is genuinely rooted — and there is a difference.

elements.text3: Part 3 — Two Men Who Prayed

Jesus told a parable specifically about two men who came before God — and about what their inner orientation in prayer revealed about the true state of their hearts. It is one of the most searching parables he told, because one of the two men is doing everything right by any outward measure. He is religious. He is morally serious. He is genuinely grateful. And yet he goes home in a worse state than the other man.

elements.passageText1: 9Jesus also told this parable to some who were confident that they were righteous and looked down on everyone else. 10“Two men went up to the temple to pray, one a Pharisee and the other a tax collector. 11The Pharisee stood and prayed about himself like this: ‘God, I thank you that I am not like other people: extortionists, unrighteous people, adulterers—or even like this tax collector. 12I fast twice a week; I give a tenth of everything I get.’ 13The tax collector, however, stood far off and would not even look up to heaven, but beat his breast and said, ‘God, be merciful to me, sinner that I am!’ 14I tell you that this man went down to his home justified rather than the Pharisee. For everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, but he who humbles himself will be exalted.”

elements.header3: "God, I thank you that I am not like other people" (v11)
elements.question3: The Pharisee’s prayer is technically a prayer of thanksgiving — and most of what he says about himself is probably true. He does fast. He does tithe. His problem is not that he is lying, but that he is comparing. He measures himself against others and finds himself superior. Where does that temptation appear in ministry contexts — and how does it quietly damage the soul?

deeper.question3: Luke tells us Jesus addressed this parable to people "who trusted in themselves that they were righteous, and who looked down on others" (v9). It is possible to do this with great religious sincerity — to be genuinely devout and genuinely contemptuous at the same time. Is there anyone — a less committed church member, a pastor’s wife with a different approach — towards whom you feel something like that quiet superiority? What does this parable say to that feeling?

elements.question3: The tax collector does not list his achievements or defend his record. He simply says: "God, be merciful to me, a sinner." That is the whole prayer. Jesus says this man — not the disciplined, grateful, morally serious Pharisee — went home justified. What is it about that posture of complete dependence that makes it the foundation of a right relationship with God?

The parable ends with a principle Jesus uses several times: "Everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and the one who humbles himself will be exalted" (v14). This is not a warning about dramatic pride — it is about the orientation of the heart in ordinary religious life. What does it look like, practically, to approach God and approach people with the tax collector’s posture rather than the Pharisee’s?

deeper.question3: Ministry can produce a kind of structural pride that is easy to miss: you are always the one who gives rather than receives, always the one who knows rather than asks, always the one who leads rather than follows. How does being in ministry make it harder to sustain the tax collector’s posture — and what disciplines might help?

elements.text3: Part 4 — The Disciplines of the Hidden Life

In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus addresses three classic practices of Jewish religious life — giving, prayer, and fasting — and in each case he makes the same point: when these things are done for an audience, they lose their power. They become performance rather than encounter. The discipline that was meant to shape the interior gets redirected toward the exterior, and the soul is left hungry.

His alternative is not to stop giving, praying, or fasting. It is to do them in secret — in a place where only God sees, where there is no audience to impress and no reputation to protect.

elements.passageText1:
1“Be careful not to display your righteousness merely to be seen by people. Otherwise you have no reward with your Father in heaven. 2Thus whenever you do charitable giving, do not blow a trumpet before you, as the hypocrites do in synagogues and on streets so that people will praise them. I tell you the truth, they have their reward! 3But when you do your giving, do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing, 4so that your gift may be in secret. And your Father, who sees in secret, will reward you.

5Whenever you pray, do not be like the hypocrites, because they love to pray while standing in synagogues and on street corners so that people can see them. Truly I say to you, they have their reward! 6But whenever you pray, go into your inner room, close the door, and pray to your Father in secret. And your Father, who sees in secret, will reward you.

16When you fast, do not look sullen like the hypocrites, for they make their faces unattractive so that people will see them fasting. I tell you the truth, they have their reward! 17When you fast, anoint your head and wash your face, 18so that it will not be obvious to others when you are fasting, but only to your Father who is in secret. And your Father, who sees in secret, will reward you.

elements.question3: Jesus repeats this phrase three times — once for each discipline. It is the anchor of the whole section. The hidden life is not hidden from God — it is the life that is lived for God, and God alone. What is the difference between a spiritual practice done for God and the same practice done for an audience — even a sympathetic or admiring one?

deeper.question3: For a pastor’s wife, the boundary between private devotion and public ministry can become very blurred — prayer becomes sermon illustration, Scripture reading becomes preparation for teaching, fasting becomes a badge of spiritual seriousness. Is there a space in your life that is genuinely private — just between you and God, not material for anything else? If not, what would it take to create one?

elements.question3: Jesus says those who pray publicly to be seen "have received their reward in full" (v5) — the admiration of others is all they will get. The person who prays in secret receives something different: an encounter with "your Father." What is lost when the practice of prayer shifts from encounter with God toward performance for people?

Fasting (vv16-18) is the most privately practiced of the three disciplines — and yet Jesus imagines people making it visible, "disfiguring their faces" so others notice they are fasting. The temptation to make even private suffering visible, for the sympathy or respect it brings, is a very human one. Where does that temptation appear in your own life — not necessarily in fasting, but in the broader instinct to perform suffering or sacrifice for an audience?

deeper.question3: The hidden disciplines — private prayer, unseen generosity, quiet fasting — are the ones that shape character most deeply, precisely because they have no external reward. They are purely between you and God. What is one hidden discipline you could begin or recover this week — something no one else would know about?

elements.term3: What is actually in the cup?
elements.question3: Jesus says to clean the inside of the cup. Before we can clean it, we need to know what is in it. What is a useful way to examine the state of your own interior life?
elements.answer3:
Honest self-examination is not the same as self-criticism. It is not about cataloguing failures or talking yourself into shame. It is simply about asking: What is actually true about my inner life right now?

Some useful questions to sit with:

  • What do I find myself thinking about most, when no one is watching?
  • What emotions am I regularly suppressing or avoiding?
  • When I serve, what do I most want in return — and what does that tell me?
  • Is there someone I have not forgiven, or a resentment I have stopped examining?
  • What do I do when I am tired and no one is looking — and what does that reveal?
  • When I am alone with God, is that a relief or an awkwardness?

These questions are not meant to produce anxiety. They are meant to produce honesty — the kind of honesty that makes it possible to bring the real you to God, rather than the managed version.

The tax collector did not examine himself extensively. He simply stopped hiding. That is the starting point.

elements.instruction3: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements3: I have a genuine private devotional life that is separate from my ministry preparation.
I find it easy to be honest with God about my real inner state — not just my public face.
I am aware of patterns of resentment, pride, or bitterness that I need to bring to God.
I spend more energy on how I appear to the congregation than on who I am before God.
I regularly practise some form of honest self-examination or spiritual reflection.

elements.text3: Personal Reflection

elements.question3: Chisom, in our opening story, had lost track of what was underneath the surface. If you were to describe honestly what is currently underneath your own surface — the things that come out on Sunday evening, or when you are tired and no one is watching — what would you say?
Of the passages in this session — 1 Samuel 16, Matthew 23, Luke 18, Matthew 6 — which one felt most personal, most searching, or most uncomfortable? What do you think that discomfort is pointing to?
What is one specific, practical step you could take this week to tend your hidden life — something between you and God alone, with no audience and no reward except the encounter itself?

elements.text3:
The congregation sees your Sunday. God sees your Tuesday. Your Sunday can be good and real and genuinely honouring to God — but it cannot stay that way indefinitely if Tuesday is empty.

The hidden life is not a bonus for especially spiritual people. It is the root system. Everything that is visible above the ground depends on what is happening underneath it, in the dark, unseen. A tree with no root system can look healthy for a season — but it cannot stand when the wind comes.

You are in ministry for the long haul. The wind will come. What you tend now, in the hidden places — in the quiet, in the honest prayer, in the private disciplines that no one sees — that is what will hold you.

God sees in secret. And what he sees, he tends.

elements.question3: What is your challenge for this week?
elements.answer3:
Set aside fifteen minutes this week for what you might call a quiet audit — not a time of intercession or preparation, but a time of honest sitting before God.

Use the questions from the callout in Part 4 as a starting point. Ask God to show you what is actually in the cup — not to condemn you, but to begin the work of cleaning from the inside.

Then take one thing you find — one emotion, one pattern, one unexamined corner — and simply name it before God. You do not need to fix it in one session. You just need to stop pretending it is not there.

The tax collector’s prayer is always available: God, be merciful to me, a sinner. It is the prayer that goes home justified.

Bring one honest reflection from your week — not a polished one — to the start of Session 5.

elements.question1: It’s worth having these passages open as you work through this session.

elements.text1:
Amina had been in ministry long enough to know that no one talked about this part.

The sermons preached in their church were full of faith and victory. The testimony meetings rang with stories of breakthrough and blessing. The women in the fellowship smiled and praised God and spoke of his goodness — and it was all true, as far as it went.

But Amina was in her third year of what she could only describe as a dry season. Not a crisis — nothing dramatic had happened. Her husband’s church plant was growing slowly, much more slowly than they had hoped. Two families they had poured themselves into had left without explanation. A woman she had discipled for eighteen months had turned against her after a misunderstanding that still made no sense. She had been sleeping badly for months. She had stopped enjoying prayer. She felt, most days, like she was going through the motions — and she felt guilty for feeling that way, because she knew she was supposed to feel grateful.

She had not told anyone. Pastors’ wives don’t say these things. Or so she had been taught, by everything she had observed around her.

This session says otherwise. Difficulty, dryness, loneliness, and grief are not signs of weak faith. They are part of the landscape of a life faithfully lived — and the Bible is far more honest about them than most churches are.
Part 1 — The Psalms Give Us Permission

The book of Psalms is the prayer book of the Bible — used by Israel in worship for centuries, quoted by Jesus on the cross, sung in the early church. It is also, startlingly, full of complaint, lament, confusion, and raw grief. Roughly a third of the psalms are laments — prayers that begin not with praise, but with a question or a cry.

The fact that these prayers are in the Bible is itself a statement: God is not offended by honest distress. He is not embarrassed by our confusion. He does not require us to perform contentment we do not feel. He invites the real thing.

Psalm 13 is one of the shortest laments in the Psalter. It is also one of the most complete, moving in just six verses from desolation to trust — not because the circumstances have changed, but because the psalmist has brought them honestly before God.

elements.passageText1:
1How long, Lord, will you continue to ignore me? How long will you pay no attention to me? 2How long must I worry, and suffer in broad daylight? How long will my enemy gloat over me? 3Look at me! Answer me, O Lord my God! Revive me, or else I will die. 4Then my enemy will say, “I have defeated him.” Then my foes will rejoice because I am shaken.

5But I trust in your faithfulness. May I rejoice because of your deliverance. 6I will sing praises to the Lord when he vindicates me.

elements.question1: The psalmist asks “How long?” four times in two verses. It is a cry of exhaustion as much as a question. He feels forgotten, hidden from, left alone with his sorrow. Have you ever felt something like this — a sense that God has gone quiet, or that your prayers are going nowhere? What did that feel like?

deeper.question1: The psalmist does not soften his language or add theological qualifications — he simply says what he feels. There is no “I know you haven’t really forgotten me, but…” He just says: “How long will you forget me?” What does it tell you about God that this kind of raw, unfiltered prayer is not only permitted but preserved in Scripture — given to every generation to pray?

elements.question1: Between verse 2 and verse 5, something shifts — not in the psalmist’s circumstances, but in his orientation. He moves from “how long?” to “I trust in your faithful love.” Nothing has changed externally. What do you think happened in the act of bringing the complaint to God? What does lament do that suppressed suffering cannot?

The psalm ends in verse 6 with praise — not triumphant, but quiet: “I will sing to the Lord, for he has been good to me.” This is not denial of difficulty. It is memory — recalling what God has done before, as a foundation for trust in the present. What has God done for you — in the past, even the distant past — that you can hold onto in a dry or difficult season now?

deeper.question1: Many Christians, and many pastors’ wives especially, have never learned to lament — to bring grief and confusion to God in honest, unpolished language. They move straight from distress to praise, skipping the honest middle. What do you think is lost when we skip the lament? And what might it look like for you to practise it?

elements.term1: “If I were stronger in faith, I wouldn’t feel this way.”
elements.question1: Is depression, exhaustion, or spiritual dryness a sign of weak faith?
elements.answer1:
This is one of the most damaging things believed in many Nigerian church contexts — and it is not supported by the Bible.

Elijah, one of the greatest prophets in Israel’s history, sat under a tree after his greatest ministry victory and asked God to take his life (1 Kings 19:4). He was exhausted, afraid, and done. God’s response was not rebuke. It was food, water, sleep, and the gentle question: “What are you doing here, Elijah?” — asked not in judgment, but in care.

David, described as “a man after God’s own heart,” wrote Psalms of anguish, confusion, and despair. Jeremiah is sometimes called the weeping prophet. Paul described being “under great pressure, far beyond our ability to endure, so that we despaired of life itself” (2 Corinthians 1:8). These are not people of weak faith. They are among the most faithful people in the entire Bible.

Mental and emotional exhaustion are not spiritual failures. They are human realities — realities that God meets with compassion, not condemnation. If you are struggling with persistent low mood, exhaustion that sleep does not fix, loss of pleasure in things that once brought joy, or thoughts that frighten you, please speak to someone — a trusted friend, a pastor, or a medical professional. These things deserve care, not concealment.

elements.text1: Part 2 — Jars of Clay

Paul writes 2 Corinthians from a position of considerable suffering. He has been beaten, imprisoned, shipwrecked, and rejected. He is writing to a church that has questioned his authority and doubted his apostleship — partly, it seems, because he does not look impressive enough. He is not a polished orator. He carries the marks of a hard life. He does not project strength.

His response is one of the most remarkable passages in the New Testament — not a defence of his credentials, but a reframing of what ministry actually is. Weakness, he argues, is not an embarrassment to the gospel. It is the condition under which the gospel is most clearly displayed.

elements.passageText1:
7But we have this treasure in clay jars, so that the extraordinary power belongs to God and does not come from us. 8We are experiencing trouble on every side, but are not crushed; we are perplexed, but not driven to despair; 9we are persecuted, but not abandoned; we are knocked down, but not destroyed, 10always carrying around in our body the death of Jesus, so that the life of Jesus may also be made visible in our body. 11For we who are alive are constantly being handed over to death for Jesus’ sake, so that the life of Jesus may also be made visible in our mortal body. 12As a result, death is at work in us, but life is at work in you.

13But since we have the same spirit of faith as that shown in what has been written, “I believed; therefore I spoke,” we also believe, therefore we also speak. 14We do so because we know that the one who raised up Jesus will also raise us up with Jesus and will bring us with you into his presence. 15For all these things are for your sake, so that the grace that is including more and more people may cause thanksgiving to increase to the glory of God. 16Therefore we do not despair, but even if our physical body is wearing away, our inner person is being renewed day by day. 17For our momentary, light suffering is producing for us an eternal weight of glory far beyond all comparison 18because we are not looking at what can be seen but at what cannot be seen. For what can be seen is temporary, but what cannot be seen is eternal.

elements.question1: Paul says the treasure of the gospel is held in “jars of clay” — ordinary, fragile, breakable vessels. He says this is deliberate: “so that the surpassing greatness of the power may be from God and not from us.” Why might God choose to work through fragile, ordinary, visibly imperfect people rather than through impressive, invulnerable ones?

deeper.question1: The expectation in many church cultures — and particularly the expectation on pastors’ wives — is to project strength, composure, and invulnerability. Paul’s theology runs directly against this. What would it look like for a pastor’s wife to embody the “jar of clay” principle — to be genuinely useful to God through her fragility rather than despite it?

elements.question1: Paul lists four pairs of contrasts in verses 8-9: pressed but not crushed; perplexed but not in despair; persecuted but not abandoned; struck down but not destroyed. Each one acknowledges the real difficulty while insisting the final word has not yet been spoken. Which of these four pairs resonates most with your own experience right now — and why?

Verse 17 is startling: Paul calls his suffering “momentary and light” — and yet he has just listed beatings, imprisonments, and despair of life itself. He is not minimising the pain. He is comparing it — to “an eternal weight of glory far beyond all comparison.” What does it do to present suffering to hold it alongside that comparison? And is that comparison dishonest, or is it the most honest thing of all?

deeper.question1: Verse 18 says Paul fixes his eyes “not on what is seen, but on what is unseen.” This is not escapism — he has just spent eleven verses describing his suffering in very concrete terms. It is a deliberate act of reorientation. What practices help you to keep what is unseen in view, during seasons when what is seen is painful or discouraging?

elements.text1: Part 3 — Elijah Under the Tree

The story of Elijah’s collapse in 1 Kings 19 is one of the most psychologically honest passages in the Old Testament. It follows immediately after one of the greatest acts of prophetic ministry in Israel’s history — the confrontation with the prophets of Baal on Mount Carmel (1 Kings 18). Elijah has just experienced extraordinary power and extraordinary vindication.

And then Jezebel sends a single threatening message, and he falls apart.

This sequence matters. Burnout, collapse, and despair do not always follow failure. They often follow great effort — sometimes the greatest effort. The crash after the mountain is a real pattern, and God’s response to it in this passage is one of the most tender things in all of Scripture.

elements.passageText1:
1Ahab told Jezebel all that Elijah had done, including a detailed account of how he killed all the prophets with the sword. 2Jezebel sent a messenger to Elijah with this warning, “May the gods judge me severely if by this time tomorrow I do not take your life as you did theirs!”

3Elijah was afraid, so he got up and fled for his life to Beer Sheba in Judah. He left his servant there, 4while he went a day’s journey into the wilderness. He went and sat down under a shrub and asked the Lord to take his life: “I’ve had enough! Now, O Lord, take my life. After all, I’m no better than my ancestors.” 5He stretched out and fell asleep under the shrub. Suddenly an angelic messenger touched him and said, “Get up and eat.” 6He looked and right there by his head was a cake baking on hot coals and a jug of water. He ate and drank and then slept some more. 7The angel of the Lord came back again, touched him, and said, “Get up and eat, for otherwise you won’t be able to make the journey.” 8So he got up and ate and drank. That meal gave him the strength to travel forty days and forty nights until he reached Horeb, the mountain of God.

9He went into a cave there and spent the night. Suddenly the Lord’s message came to him, “Why are you here, Elijah?” 10He answered, “I have been absolutely loyal to the Lord God of Heaven’s Armies, even though the Israelites have abandoned the covenant they made with you, torn down your altars, and killed your prophets with the sword. I alone am left and now they want to take my life.” 11The Lord said, “Go out and stand on the mountain before the Lord. Look, the Lord is ready to pass by.”

A very powerful wind went before the Lord, digging into the mountain and causing landslides, but the Lord was not in the wind. After the windstorm there was an earthquake, but the Lord was not in the earthquake. 12After the earthquake, there was a fire, but the Lord was not in the fire. After the fire, there was a soft whisper. 13When Elijah heard it, he covered his face with his robe and went out and stood at the entrance to the cave. Suddenly a voice asked him, “Why are you here, Elijah?” 14He answered, “I have been absolutely loyal to the Lord God of Heaven’s Armies, even though the Israelites have abandoned the covenant they made with you, torn down your altars, and killed your prophets with the sword. I alone am left and now they want to take my life.”

15The Lord said to him, “Go back the way you came and then head for the wilderness of Damascus. Go and anoint Hazael king over Syria. 16You must anoint Jehu son of Nimshi king over Israel, and Elisha son of Shaphat from Abel Meholah to take your place as prophet. 17Jehu will kill anyone who escapes Hazael’s sword, and Elisha will kill anyone who escapes Jehu’s sword. 18I still have left in Israel 7,000 followers who have not bowed their knees to Baal or kissed the images of him.

elements.question1: Elijah asks God to take his life: “I have had enough, Lord.” He is not being dramatic — he is exhausted beyond his capacity to continue. Have you ever reached a point like that — not necessarily as extreme, but a point where you genuinely felt you could not keep going? What brought you there?

deeper.question1: Elijah’s collapse comes immediately after a great ministry victory. Is there a pattern in your own life — periods of intense giving or effort followed by unexpected flatness or crash? What do you think is happening in those moments, and what do you most need from God and from others when they come?

elements.question1: God’s first response to Elijah’s despair is not theology, not correction, and not a call back to ministry. It is food and sleep — twice (vv5-8). He attends to the body before he addresses the soul. What does this say about how God views the relationship between physical wellbeing and spiritual health? And what does it say to a culture that sometimes treats rest and physical care as signs of spiritual laziness?

When God finally speaks to Elijah (v13), he asks: “What are you doing here, Elijah?” — and Elijah gives the same answer twice (vv10, 14), almost word for word. He is stuck. He cannot see past his own narrative of isolation and failure: “I alone am left.” God’s response (vv15-18) does not argue with the feeling — he simply gives Elijah a next step, and quietly corrects the isolation: “I have kept seven thousand in Israel.” What does this approach — a gentle next step rather than a rebuke — tell you about how God handles people who are depleted and stuck?

deeper.question1: “I alone am left” is a feeling that many pastors’ wives know — the sense of being uniquely isolated, uniquely burdened, uniquely unseen. It is rarely literally true, but it feels true. What would it mean for you to hear God’s quiet correction of that narrative — “There are seven thousand you don’t know about” — applied to your own situation?

elements.term1: “Pastors’ wives don’t talk about these things.”
elements.question1: What happens when there is no safe space for a pastor’s wife to be honest about her struggles?
elements.answer1:
In many church contexts — particularly in traditional or hierarchical settings — there is an unwritten rule that those in leadership do not show weakness. A pastor’s wife who admits to loneliness, depression, marital strain, or spiritual dryness risks being seen as a liability to her husband’s ministry, a disappointment to the congregation, or simply someone who “doesn’t have enough faith.”

The result is a particular kind of loneliness: being surrounded by people, and profoundly alone.

This is not a small problem. Concealed struggles do not tend to stay concealed — they grow. And when they eventually surface, they often do so in ways that are far more damaging than honest disclosure would have been.

The Bible models something very different. Paul shares his anguish with the churches he serves. The psalmists publish their laments. Elijah’s collapse is not hidden in a footnote — it is given an entire chapter. The message is consistent: honesty before God and before trusted others is not weakness. It is the condition of genuine health.

Every pastor’s wife deserves at least one person — ideally outside her own congregation — to whom she can say the real thing and be received without judgment. If you do not have that person, finding one is not a luxury. It is a necessity.

elements.text1: Part 4 — The God of All Comfort

Paul opens 2 Corinthians — the letter in which he is most honest about his own suffering — not with complaint, but with a doxology. Before he describes what he has been through, he names who God has been to him in it. And the name he reaches for is not “God of power” or “God of victory.” It is something quieter and more personal: the God of all comfort.

What follows is not just a personal testimony. It is a theology of suffering — an account of why difficulty, when it is met by God’s comfort, does not merely end in relief, but becomes a resource for others.

elements.passageText1: 3Blessed is the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of mercies and God of all comfort, 4who comforts us in all our troubles so that we may be able to comfort those experiencing any trouble with the comfort with which we ourselves are comforted by God. 5For just as the sufferings of Christ overflow toward us, so also our comfort through Christ overflows to you. 6But if we are afflicted, it is for your comfort and salvation; if we are comforted, it is for your comfort that you experience in your patient endurance of the same sufferings that we also suffer. 7And our hope for you is steadfast because we know that as you share in our sufferings, so also you will share in our comfort.

elements.question1: Paul does not say God comforts us in some things or most things — he says “all comfort” comes from God. That is an enormous claim. What does it mean to locate all comfort in God — and does it exclude human comfort, practical help, or rest? How do you understand the relationship between God’s comfort and the comfort that comes through other people?

Verse 4 contains one of the most remarkable statements in Paul’s letters: God comforts us “so that we may be able to comfort those who are in any affliction with the comfort with which we ourselves are comforted by God.” Comfort is not meant to stop with us — it flows through us to others. Has there been a difficulty in your own life that has made you better able to come alongside someone else in a similar difficulty? What did that look like?

deeper.question1: Paul’s theology here suggests that the hard things in your own story — the loneliness, the criticism, the exhaustion, the grief — are not wasted. They become the material from which genuine pastoral care is made. How does that reframe the way you think about your own suffering? Does it change anything about how you hold it?

elements.question1: Verse 7 speaks of sharing in both suffering and comfort — a community of those who have been through things together and have been met by God together. That requires honesty about the suffering, not just celebration of the comfort. What would a community of pastors’ wives look like if it was genuinely characterised by this kind of shared honesty — not just mutual encouragement, but mutual lament and mutual comfort?

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements1: I feel free to be honest about my struggles — with God, and with at least one other person.
I believe that difficulty and weakness do not disqualify me from ministry.
I am currently carrying something heavy that I have not yet brought fully to God.
I have at least one person outside my congregation to whom I can say the real thing.
I can see ways in which my own hard experiences have made me more useful to others.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: Amina, in our opening story, had learned that pastors’ wives don’t say these things. Has this session given you permission to say them — to God, or to someone else? What would you most need to say?
Of the four passages in this session — Psalm 13, 2 Corinthians 4, 1 Kings 19, 2 Corinthians 1 — which one came closest to naming something true about your own experience? What was it?

Paul says God comforts us so that we can comfort others with the same comfort. Is there something in your own story of difficulty — even something still in progress — that God might already be using, or preparing to use, for someone else’s good? How do you feel about that possibility?

elements.text1:
You are not the first pastor’s wife to sit under a tree and say: It is enough. I have had enough.

You are not the first to feel forgotten, or to pray and hear nothing, or to give and give and find yourself running on empty. You are not the first to smile on Sunday and weep on Tuesday. You are not the first to wonder whether anyone sees what this actually costs.

Elijah was not the first either. And God did not rebuke him. He fed him, let him sleep, and asked a quiet question. Then he sent him back — not to the same thing, but to the next thing. And it turned out he was not as alone as he thought.

You are not as alone as you think. There are seven thousand you don’t know about. And the God who fed Elijah under the juniper tree, who comforted Paul in his imprisonments, who received the psalmist’s raw and unpolished grief — that God sees you. All of you. Even the parts you have been afraid to show.

Especially those parts.

elements.question1: What is your challenge for this week?
elements.answer1:
Two things this week — and you choose the order.

First: write a lament. Not a polished prayer — a real one. Take a page and write to God about whatever is actually hard right now. Use the structure of Psalm 13 if it helps: How long…? / Look on me and answer… / But I trust… You do not have to reach the trust part in one sitting. The point is to say the real thing.

Second: identify one person — outside your congregation if possible — to whom you could say something true about how you are actually doing. Not the full story, necessarily. Just something real. A text, a phone call, a cup of tea. The beginning of the conversation Amina had never allowed herself to have.

Bring whatever you are willing to share from these two things to the start of Session 6.

elements.question1: It’s worth having these passages open as you work through this session.

elements.text1:
Funke had not taken a full day off in four years.

She would have found the observation strange, if anyone had made it — because in her mind, she was not overworking. She was just being faithful. There was always something: a hospital visit, a woman who needed prayer, a children’s programme to organise, a meal to cook for a grieving family, a sermon to type up, a meeting to attend alongside her husband. And when there was nothing scheduled, there was the low hum of availability — the sense that she should be ready, at any moment, to be useful.

She did not call it exhaustion. She called it service.

But her body was beginning to say otherwise. She had started forgetting things — small things, but more often than before. She snapped at her children over nothing. She sat in church on Sunday mornings and felt, beneath the singing, a numbness she did not know what to do with. She had begun to dread the week ahead on Sunday evening rather than greeting it with anything like eagerness.

If someone had suggested she needed rest — real rest, not just an earlier bedtime — she would have said: But the work is not done. It is never done.

And that, it turns out, is precisely the point.
Part 1 — God Rested

The concept of sabbath — of a rhythm of work and rest built into the fabric of existence — does not begin with the Ten Commandments. It begins at the very beginning, in the first two chapters of the Bible, before the Fall, before sin, before human failure of any kind. God rested on the seventh day and called it holy. That pattern was written into creation before anything went wrong.

This means that rest is not a concession to human weakness. It is not a reluctant accommodation to our limitations. It is part of the original design — woven into the structure of time itself by God, who could have kept working and did not.

elements.passageText1: 1The heavens and the earth were completed with everything that was in them. 2By the seventh day God finished the work that he had been doing, and he ceased on the seventh day all the work that he had been doing. 3God blessed the seventh day and made it holy because on it he ceased all the work that he had been doing in creation.

elements.question1: God did not rest because he was tired. He rested because the work was complete — and because completion deserves to be marked, savoured, acknowledged. What does it say about the nature of rest that God himself practised it, before humanity ever existed? And what does it say about the value of rest that God blessed the seventh day and called it holy?

deeper.question1: The work of ministry — unlike the work of creation — is never quite complete. There is always another visit to make, another prayer to pray, another person who needs something. How do you build a rhythm of rest into a life where the work genuinely never ends? What would it mean to rest not because the work is finished, but because the rhythm is holy?

elements.question1: When the sabbath commandment is given in Exodus 20:8-11, it is grounded in creation: "for in six days the Lord made the heavens and the earth… and he rested on the seventh day." When it is repeated in Deuteronomy 5:12-15, it is grounded in the Exodus — in liberation from slavery. In Egypt, slaves did not rest. Rest, in God’s economy, is something free people do. What does it mean that rest is a sign of freedom — and what does it say about a life that has no room for it?

deeper.question1: Many women in ministry feel that taking rest is selfish — that while they are resting, someone somewhere is not being cared for. Is that feeling correct? And where do you think it comes from — Scripture, culture, personality, or something else?

elements.term1: "Rest is for people who are not serious about the work."
elements.question1: Is taking regular rest a sign of insufficient commitment to ministry?
elements.answer1:
This idea is common in high-commitment ministry cultures — and it is not from the Bible.

Jesus, in the middle of active and demanding ministry, regularly withdrew to lonely places to pray (Luke 5:16). He slept in a boat during a storm (Mark 4:38). He told his disciples, after an intense period of ministry activity, to ‘come away by yourselves to a desolate place and rest a while’ (Mark 6:31) — and this instruction came because so many people were coming and going that they did not even have time to eat. Busyness was not Jesus’ reason to press harder. It was his reason to withdraw.

Elijah needed sleep and food before he could hear from God (1 Kings 19). The psalmist described being led ‘beside still waters’ and having his soul restored (Psalm 23:2-3). These are not pictures of laziness. They are pictures of a God who builds restoration into the rhythm of a faithful life.

The person who never rests is not more committed than the person who does. They are less sustainable — and over time, less useful. The work of ministry is a long-distance endeavour. It is not run at sprint pace. Those who attempt to sprint it tend not to finish well.

Rest is not the enemy of faithfulness. It is the condition of it.

elements.text1: Part 2 — The Shepherd Who Restores

Psalm 23 is perhaps the most familiar psalm in the entire Psalter — so familiar that it can be difficult to hear it freshly. But it contains one of the most striking images of God’s care in all of Scripture: a shepherd who leads his sheep to still water and green pasture, not because the sheep have earned it, but because that is what a good shepherd does.

The word translated ‘restores’ in verse 3 — yeshobeb in Hebrew — means to bring back, to cause to return, to turn around. It is the word used of someone who has wandered and is brought home. God does not restore us because we maintained the pace. He restores us because we are his, and restoration is what he does.

elements.passageText1: 1The Lord is my shepherd, I lack nothing. 2He takes me to lush pastures, he leads me to refreshing water. 3He restores my strength. He leads me down the right paths for the sake of his reputation. 4Even when I must walk through the darkest valley, I fear no danger, for you are with me; your rod and your staff reassure me. 5You prepare a feast before me in plain sight of my enemies. You refresh my head with oil; my cup is completely full. 6Surely your goodness and faithfulness will pursue me all my days, and I will live in the Lord’s house for the rest of my life.

elements.question1: Green pastures and still waters are images of provision, peace, and unhurried nourishment. They are not images of productivity. The shepherd does not lead his sheep to a place where they can do more — he leads them to a place where they can simply be. When did you last experience anything like that — a season or even a moment of genuine, unhurried rest? What made it possible?

deeper.question1: The sheep do not find the green pasture by themselves. The shepherd leads them there. Is it possible that God has been trying to lead you toward rest — through tiredness, through circumstances that have slowed you down, through this study — and that you have been resisting? What would it mean to follow where he is leading?

elements.question1: Verse 3 says: "He restores my soul." Not: "I restore my soul by working harder." Not: "My soul is restored when I have done enough." Restoration is something God does to us, not something we achieve for ourselves. What does this imply about what it means to receive rest from God — and what does it demand from us in return?

The psalm moves through green pasture and still water (v2), through the valley of the shadow of death (v4), and arrives at a prepared table — a feast in the presence of enemies (v5). Rest and restoration do not mean the absence of difficulty. They mean the presence of the shepherd through it. How does that change what you are looking for when you seek rest — from circumstances, or from God?

deeper.question1: The psalm ends not with exhaustion averted, but with goodness and mercy following the psalmist "all the days of my life" — including the hard ones, including the valley. The rest God gives is not a holiday from reality. It is a deep settledness that can hold both the green pasture and the dark valley. Do you have that kind of settledness? If not, what do you think is in the way?

elements.text1: Part 3 — Come to Me

In Matthew 11, Jesus has just been speaking about the rejection of John the Baptist and the resistance of the cities that have seen his miracles. It is a chapter of disappointment and hard-heartedness. And in the middle of it, he turns and offers one of the most direct and personal invitations in the Gospels — addressed not to a crowd, not to the Pharisees, but to anyone who is weary and carrying a heavy load.

The invitation is not to try harder. It is to come.

elements.passageText1: 28Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. 29Take my yoke on you and learn from me, because I am gentle and humble in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. 30For my yoke is easy to bear, and my load is not hard to carry.
elements.passageText2: <sup>28</sup>Ku zo gare ni dukanku masu wahala, masu fama da kaya, ni kuwa zan hutasshe ku. <sup>29</sup>11.29 Irm 6.16 Ku shiga bautata, ku kuma koya a gare ni, domin ni mai tawali’u ne, marar girmankai, za ku kuwa sami kwanciyar rai. <sup>30</sup>Domin bautata sassauƙa ce, kayana kuma marar nauyi ne.”
elements.passageText3: <sup>28</sup>“Ku zo gare ni, dukanku da kuka gaji, kuma kuna fama da kaya masu nauyi a kanku, zan ba ku hutawa. <sup>29</sup>Ku miƙa kanku ga umarnaina kamar yadda saniya takan miƙa wuyanta ga gungumen noma, ku kuma koya daga wurina, domin ni mai tawaliˈu ne, mai sauƙin kai, kuma za ku sami hutu a zuciyarku. 11.29 Irm 6.16 <sup>30</sup>Gama gungumena yana da sauƙi, umarnaina kuma ba nauyi.”

elements.header1: "Come to me, all who are weary" (v28)
elements.question1: Jesus does not say ‘Come to me when you have sorted yourself out’ or ‘Come to me once you have finished your responsibilities.’ He says come now — as you are, burdened as you are. What is the burden you are currently carrying that he is addressing in this invitation? What would it mean to actually bring it to him?

deeper.question1: The word translated "weary" (kopiao) in verse 28 refers to weariness from labour — the tiredness of someone who has been working hard. Jesus is not speaking to the spiritually complacent. He is speaking to the over-committed, the over-extended, the people who have been giving everything and have run out. Does it matter to you that this invitation is specifically for people in that condition? What does it do to the invitation to know it is addressed to you?

elements.question1: Jesus offers "rest for your souls" (v29) — not just rest for your body, not just a lighter schedule, but something deeper: rest at the level of the soul. He says this rest comes through taking his yoke — learning from him, being joined to him in the work. What is the difference between the burden of ministry carried in your own strength, and the same ministry carried in his yoke? What does the yoke of Jesus actually feel like, when you are in it?

Jesus describes himself as "gentle and lowly in heart" (v29). This is a remarkable self-description — not powerful, not impressive, not authoritative, but gentle and humble. What does it do to your willingness to come to him when you know that this is what he is like — that the one you are coming to is not impatient with your weakness, but gentle toward it?

deeper.question1: The yoke Jesus offers is "easy" and his burden is "light" — but he has already told his disciples they must take up their cross and follow him (Matthew 16:24). He is not promising a life without weight. He is promising a different kind of weight — one shared, one borne alongside him. How does the presence of Jesus in the yoke change the nature of the burden, even when the circumstances remain hard?

elements.term1: "I can rest when the children are grown / the church is bigger / things are more settled."
elements.question1: Is it wise to defer rest until circumstances improve?
elements.answer1:
The circumstances of ministry rarely settle in the way we imagine they will. The season of young children passes, and is replaced by the season of adolescent children. The small church plant grows, and with it the demands multiply rather than diminish. The hoped-for stability turns out to be another season of growth or difficulty with different contours.

There is no future version of your life in which rest will be easier to justify than it is now. The person who does not rest now will not suddenly find it natural to rest then. Patterns of overwork, like patterns of any kind, tend to deepen with time rather than resolve themselves.

The call to rest is not a call to wait for the right season. It is a call to build the rhythm now — imperfectly, with whatever margin you can find, in whatever form is available to you. A full sabbath day may not be possible every week in every season. But something — an hour, a morning, a practice of stopping — is almost always possible, if we are willing to treat it as the priority it actually is.

Start small. Start now. The pattern, once begun, becomes easier to protect.

elements.text1: Part 4 — Abiding, Not Striving

John 15 contains Jesus’ final extended teaching to his disciples before his arrest. He knows what is coming. And what he chooses to teach in this moment — his last opportunity for sustained instruction — is not a strategy for mission, not a plan for the church, not a list of responsibilities. It is a picture of a relationship.

He is the vine. They are the branches. The branch does not produce fruit by striving harder. It produces fruit by remaining — by staying connected to the source. The Greek word meno, translated ‘remain’ or ‘abide,’ appears ten times in the first ten verses of this passage. It is the keynote of Jesus’ final teaching.

Stay. Abide. Remain. Everything else follows from that.

elements.passageText1:
1“I am the true vine and my Father is the gardener. 2He takes away every branch that does not bear fruit in me. He prunes every branch that bears fruit so that it will bear more fruit. 3You are clean already because of the word that I have spoken to you. 4Remain in me, and I will remain in you. Just as the branch cannot bear fruit by itself, unless it remains in the vine, so neither can you unless you remain in me.

5“I am the vine; you are the branches. The one who remains in me—and I in him—bears much fruit, because apart from me you can accomplish nothing. 6If anyone does not remain in me, he is thrown out like a branch, and dries up; and such branches are gathered up and thrown into the fire, and are burned up. 7If you remain in me and my words remain in you, ask whatever you want, and it will be done for you. 8My Father is honored by this, that you bear much fruit and show that you are my disciples.

9“Just as the Father has loved me, I have also loved you; remain in my love. 10If you obey my commandments, you will remain in my love, just as I have obeyed my Father’s commandments and remain in his love. 11I have told you these things so that my joy may be in you, and your joy may be complete.

elements.header1: "Remain in me, and I will remain in you" (v4)
elements.question1: A branch that is separated from the vine does not simply produce less fruit — it produces none, and eventually withers entirely (v6). The branch’s fruitfulness is entirely dependent on its connection to the vine, not on its own effort. How does this image challenge the way you think about your ministry output — the fruit you produce, the people you serve, the work you do?

deeper.question1: Jesus says "apart from me you can do nothing" (v5) — not less, not little, but nothing of lasting value. That is a strong claim. But he also says that the branch that remains in him will bear "much fruit." What is the practical difference, in your daily life, between a day lived from connection to the vine and a day lived from your own effort and momentum? What does each feel like?

elements.question1: Verse 2 mentions pruning — the Father removing branches that don’t bear fruit, and cutting back the ones that do, so they bear more. Pruning is not punishment. It is the removal of what is draining life away so that what remains can flourish. Is there something in your current ministry life — a commitment, a role, a responsibility — that may need to be pruned? What would it take to let it go?

Jesus says he has told his disciples these things "so that my joy may be in you, and your joy may be complete" (v11). Fruitfulness, in Jesus’ vision, is not grim or joyless. It is the overflow of a life that is genuinely connected to him. When did you last experience something like that — joy in ministry that felt like overflow rather than output? What were the conditions that made it possible?

deeper.question1: Abiding is not passive — it is an active, daily choice to remain connected. For Jesus’ disciples, it meant staying close to him, listening to his words, keeping his commands (v10). For you, in practical terms, what does abiding look like? What are the specific practices, rhythms, and choices that keep you connected to the vine — especially in the seasons when connection feels difficult?

elements.term1: What does rest actually look like?
elements.question1: Rest sounds simple — but for many women in ministry, the concept is so unfamiliar that they genuinely do not know what it would mean for them. What are some practical starting points?
elements.answer1:
Rest is not the same thing for everyone, and it is not only about sleep — though sleep matters enormously and is often the first thing sacrificed in ministry life.

Rest, in the fullest sense, is whatever genuinely restores you — whatever allows you to return to people and to God with more rather than less. For some women that is solitude and silence. For others it is time with close friends, or physical activity, or creative work, or time in nature. For many it is a combination.

Some starting points worth considering:

Sabbath — a regular, weekly rhythm of stopping. Not catching up on other tasks, not being available, not doing ministry work. Simply stopping. This looks different in different households, but the principle is the same: one day in seven belongs to rest, to God, and to renewal.

Sleep — most adults need seven to nine hours. Chronically short sleep impairs judgment, emotional regulation, and creativity in ways that directly affect both ministry and marriage. Protecting sleep is not indulgence — it is stewardship.

Margin — intentional empty space in the diary. Not every hour needs to be filled. Not every request needs a yes. The ability to say no to good things in order to protect the capacity for the most important things is a discipline, not a failure.

Delight — doing things simply because they are good and beautiful and enjoyable, with no ministry application. Reading for pleasure. Cooking a meal you love. Sitting outside in the evening. God made a world full of good things to be received with gratitude (1 Timothy 4:4-5). Receiving them is not a distraction from worship. It is a form of it.

elements.instruction1: Choose the option that best reflects how well you agree or disagree with each statement.
statements1: I have a regular rhythm of rest that I actively protect.
I feel free to stop working without feeling guilty or anxious.
I am genuinely connected to Jesus as the source of my ministry, not just working from my own effort.
I know what restores me — and I make space for it regularly.
I believe that rest is a spiritual practice, not just a physical necessity.

elements.text1: Personal Reflection

elements.question1: Funke had not taken a full day off in four years — and had not noticed, because she called it faithfulness. Looking back over Volume 1, what has shifted in how you understand the relationship between faithfulness, identity, and rest? What do you most want to carry forward?
Jesus says his yoke is easy and his burden is light. On a scale of honest reflection: how heavy does your yoke feel right now? What do you think is in it that he did not put there?
What is one concrete, specific change you will make to your rhythm of life in the coming month — not a vague intention, but an actual decision? Name it as clearly as you can.

elements.text1:
We began Volume 1 with a question: Who are you when you are not being useful?

Six sessions later, here is what the Bible has said in answer.

You are a daughter of God, chosen before the world was made, known completely and loved without condition. You are more than a role — your identity is older and deeper than your husband’s calling, and it cannot be earned or lost by what you do or fail to do. You are a partner in marriage, not an assistant — an ezer, a woman of strength. You are a jar of clay carrying extraordinary treasure, and your fragility does not disqualify you; it is part of how the light gets out. You are permitted to lament, to struggle, to say it is enough — and to be met there by a God who feeds before he sends. And you are a branch, not a trunk: your fruitfulness comes from remaining connected to the vine, not from working harder and longer than is humanly possible.

None of this makes the work easier. The ministry is real, the demands are real, and the people who need you are real. But the one who called you is also real — and he is not asking you to be superhuman. He is asking you to be his.

You are his. Rest in that. And come back for Volume 2.

elements.question1: What does Volume 2 cover?
elements.answer1:
Volume 1 has been inward-facing: who you are in Christ, how you relate to Jesus, how you tend your marriage and your hidden life, how you survive difficulty, how you build a sustainable rhythm.

Volume 2 turns outward: how you live and minister from that foundation.

The six sessions of Volume 2 are:

  1. Different Women, Different Callings — there is no one-size-fits-all role for a pastor’s wife, and the pressure to conform to a single model is not from God.
  2. Teaching and Discipling — what it means to invest in other women, across generations and experience levels.
  3. Pastoral Heart — mourning with those who mourn, standing with the vulnerable, caring for those the church sometimes overlooks.
  4. Navigating Conflict — gossip, manipulation, extended family expectations, and being misunderstood.
  5. Family, Finances, and Freedom — the practical realities of household life in a ministry context.
  6. For the Long Haul — finishing well, handing on what you have learned, and the grace available at every stage.

The foundation is laid. Now for the building.
